On larger screens it may become sticky within its section (staying pinned near the top of the viewport as the calendar beneath it scrolls) if desired, but visually it remains a slim, light band that doesn’t overpower the calendar.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The availability-calendar is positioned directly beneath the availability-filter-bar, separated by a 24–32px vertical gap. It is the dominant element within the availability section, occupying the full width of the central content column (about 70% of page width, max-width ~960px) and roughly 50–70% of the viewport height on desktop. Visually, it presents as a clean grid with clearly defined rows and columns, using the underlying 12-column layout for alignment. Days appear along the top, with time slots in rows, but each cell is generously sized with 8–16px internal padding to keep the density manageable. Available slots are indicated with subtle outlines or filled states, using the purple accent color sparingly for selected slots and hover states. There is clear whitespace around the calendar: 32–48px above (to the filter bar) and 32–40px below (to the booking form), reinforcing the vertical flow and preventing the grid from feeling cramped.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: The calendar scrolls as part of the main page content. If the filter bar uses sticky behavior, the calendar scrolls beneath it within the same section, but the calendar itself does not fix or float.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The earliest-slot-selector is rendered as a prominent yet compact action button inside the availability-filter-bar, aligned to the far right edge of the central content column. It shares the bar’s vertical center line and is visually separated from the filter controls on the left by at least 24px of horizontal space. The button uses the brand’s purple accent as its primary fill color, with white text in all caps, making it the highest contrast element in the filter bar. Its width is modest (around 160–200px) and height roughly 40–48px, matching other button styles on the site. The surrounding whitespace ensures it is clearly visible but not oversized, balancing the minimal aesthetic. On smaller screens, the button wraps to a second row below the filters but remains right-aligned within the same central width.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: The earliest-slot-selector moves with the availability-filter-bar. If the bar becomes sticky within its section, the button remains in its right-aligned position within that sticky band; otherwise, it scrolls naturally with the page.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The lesson-booking-form sits directly beneath the availability-calendar, separated by a 32–40px vertical gap. It is a centered panel aligned to the same central column as the calendar and panels above, spanning roughly 60–70% of the page width (max-width ~720–840px) to keep form fields within a comfortable reading width. The form appears as a card with a subtle background contrast from the main page color and 24–32px padding on all sides. Content inside is arranged in a simple vertical stack: section title at the top, then form fields (e.g., lesson type, duration, notes), followed by a prominent purple primary action button (e.g., 'Add Lesson to Cart'), each separated by 16–24px. The overall height remains moderate, avoiding overly long vertical forms; where necessary, fields are grouped logically with clear headings and adequate whitespace. When no slot is selected, the form either appears in a subdued, disabled state or collapsed to a short prompt, preserving the clean layout.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: The booking form is part of the normal scrollable flow. Its visibility and state (collapsed, expanded, active) are controlled by the page-state-manager based on calendar interactions, but it does not fix or float on the screen.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The booking-confirmation-panel appears as a modal-like overlay centered in the viewport, floating above all other content. When active, a semi-transparent dark scrim covers the entire page, dimming the hero and sections beneath while maintaining a hint of the tennis imagery and layout. The confirmation panel itself is aligned to the same central grid, with a fixed max-width of about 480–600px and 32–40px padding inside. It is vertically centered, occupying roughly the middle third of the viewport height. Inside, a small purple label at the top (e.g., 'BOOKING CONFIRMED') is followed by a bold, white or high-contrast heading and a brief summary of the booked lesson details in a tidy vertical stack with 16–24px spacing. Primary and secondary actions (e.g., 'View Cart', 'Book Another Lesson') sit at the bottom aligned either center or right, separated from the content above by a 24–32px gap. The design remains minimal and clean, with no extra chrome beyond a simple close icon aligned top-right within the panel.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: This panel is positioned fixed relative to the viewport, centered both horizontally and vertically. It overlays all other components and disables interaction with the underlying page while visible. Its visibility and transitions are controlled by the page-state-manager.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The cart-summary-widget is a compact, unobtrusive card that sits near the bottom-right corner of the viewport, floating above the main content. On desktop, it is positioned with a comfortable offset from the edges (e.g., 24–32px from the right and bottom) and has a width of roughly 280–340px, with 16–24px internal padding. The background is dark, harmonizing with the hero overlay, and features subtle purple accents for key numbers or the primary 'Go to Cart' link. The widget displays a concise summary: number of lessons, subtotal, and a small CTA, arranged in a low-density vertical stack. It does not extend higher than about 20–25% of the viewport height, ensuring the main content remains visually dominant. On smaller screens, this widget may collapse into a pill-shaped bar anchored to the bottom edge, still aligned right, to minimize obstruction.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: The cart summary is fixed to the viewport, maintaining its position as the user scrolls. It overlays the main content but with a light visual weight and can be collapsible/expandable. On mobile, its fixed bar version ensures continued access without consuming excessive vertical space.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The page-state-manager is a non-visual controller that does not occupy visible space on the page. Conceptually, it sits behind the scenes, orchestrating when components such as the lesson-booking-form, booking-confirmation-panel, error-notification-banner, and cart-summary-widget update or change state. It ensures that only relevant components are visible at any given time (e.g., showing the confirmation overlay after a successful booking, or disabling the booking form until a slot is selected), preserving the clean, minimal layout described above.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: The state manager has no direct spatial presence in the UI. It operates purely at the logic layer, controlling component visibility, transitions, and interactive states without affecting the physical layout grid.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The error-notification-banner appears as a slim horizontal strip at the very top of the page content, directly beneath the global navigation bar and above the hero’s coach-profile-header. When visible, it spans the full width of the page but keeps a low profile, with a height of about 48–64px and 12–16px vertical padding. The background is a high-contrast color (likely a dark tone with a red accent or line) consistent with the site’s minimal aesthetic. The text is short and direct, left-aligned within the central content width (matching the hero’s column) while the outer edges remain clean. There is a small close/dismiss icon aligned to the far right within this central area. The banner pushes the hero content slightly downward, but maintains at least 24–32px of breathing room between its bottom edge and the hero’s top edge, so the page doesn’t feel cramped.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: The error banner appears in the normal document flow, but may optionally be configured as sticky so it remains docked to the top of the viewport as the user scrolls. When hidden, it collapses completely, returning the hero to its original top position with no residual space.

name: Camps
title: Camps
description: Search and filter junior tennis camp sessions by age group, dates, format, and price, then open a specific camp session to view full details and enroll.

core_features: Load and display dynamic filter options for camps (age groups, months, session types) using getCampFilterOptions.
Read URL parameters to pre-populate filters (age group, month/year, date range, session type, maximum price).
Search camp sessions based on the selected filters using searchCampSessions.
Display a list of camp sessions with key details (camp name, age group label, dates, daily times, session type, price, remaining spots).
Allow users to refine filters (age group, month, date range, session type, maximum price) and immediately refresh the session list.
Show a concise summary of currently applied filters, with the ability to clear individual filters.
Provide an empty state when no sessions match the filters, with a way to reset filters.
Allow users to open a specific camp session’s detail page via click, passing camp and session identifiers in the URL.
Display a header cart summary using getCartSummary and navigate to cart.html on user request.
user_workflows:
Workflow: Landing on Camps with pre-selected filters from another page
1. User arrives on camps.html with one or more URL parameters (such as ageGroupId, month, year, sessionType, maxPrice, startDateFrom, startDateTo).
2. The page parses URL parameters into an internal filter state.
3. The page calls getCampFilterOptions to load age group, month, and session type options.
4. The filter form initializes its controls using the URL-derived filter state and matches ageGroupId and sessionType against the options from getCampFilterOptions.
5. The page normalizes date filters: if month/year are provided without explicit startDateFrom/startDateTo, it derives an appropriate date range for that month; if explicit startDateFrom/startDateTo are provided, these take precedence over month/year for the search.
6. The page calls searchCampSessions with the active filter state (ageGroupId, startDateFrom, startDateTo, month, year, sessionType, maxPrice).
7. The camp session list displays all returned sessions, grouped or sorted as defined in the component logic (client-side) without additional API calls.
8. User reviews key information (camp name, dates, times, price, remaining spots) and optionally adjusts filters to refine results.

Workflow: Refining camps by age group, dates, format, and budget
1. User interacts with the filter form (age group dropdown, month/year selector or date range inputs, session type selector, maximum price input).
2. On each filter change, the filter state is updated and synchronized into the URL query string (e.g., camps.html?ageGroupId=...&month=...&year=...&sessionType=...&maxPrice=...).
3. The page immediately calls searchCampSessions with the updated parameters.
4. The session list updates to show only the camp sessions matching the new criteria.
5. The filter summary component shows active filters as removable items. Removing an item clears the corresponding filter value, updates the URL, and triggers searchCampSessions again.
6. If the user clears all filters, the URL removes those parameters and searchCampSessions is called with undefined/empty filter values so that the backend determines the default result set.

Workflow: Browsing camp weeks and opening a specific camp session
1. After a search, the session list shows one card/row per camp_session result from searchCampSessions.
2. User scans sessions, focusing on specific start dates, session types (full day vs half day), and prices.
3. The user clicks on a session card or a dedicated "View Details" action.
4. The component reads the selected item’s camp_id and camp_session_id from the search results data.
5. The page navigates to camp_detail.html with URL parameters id={camp_id} and sessionId={camp_session_id}.
6. The camp detail page (outside the scope of this spec) uses these parameters to display full information and start enrollment.

Workflow: Using the cart summary while browsing camps
1. On page load, the header cart summary component calls getCartSummary.
2. The component displays the current number of items (derived from the returned items array) and total amount (from cart.total_amount, if provided).
3. While the user browses sessions and navigates back and forth between pages, the cart summary can be refreshed on page load or on demand (e.g., when the user returns from the cart or camp_detail pages), always using getCartSummary.
4. When the user clicks the cart summary "View Cart" action, the page navigates to cart.html without additional parameters.
interactions: On initial page load, the page parses URL parameters (ageGroupId, month, year, sessionType, maxPrice, startDateFrom, startDateTo) and stores them in an internal filter state object used by all filter-related components.
On initial page load, the page triggers getCampFilterOptions to retrieve available age_groups, months, and session_types; the filter form binds its controls to these dynamic options.
After filter options are loaded and initial filter state is established, the page triggers searchCampSessions with the current filter state.

Whenever the user changes a filter control (age group, month/year, date range, session type, maximum price), an onChange event handler updates the filter state, syncs the change to the URL query string, and re-calls searchCampSessions.

If the user selects a month and year while a custom date range is already set, the page can either: (a) overwrite the date range with a range derived from month/year, or (b) update month/year based on the chosen date range. The precedence logic is defined in the state_logic section and applied consistently.
Filter summary chips are interactive: clicking a chip’s remove icon clears that specific filter, updates the URL, and re-executes searchCampSessions.

Session list items are clickable; onClick handlers read the associated camp_id and camp_session_id from the bound data and perform a location change to camp_detail.html?id={camp_id}&sessionId={camp_session_id}.

If searchCampSessions returns an empty array, the empty state component appears. The user can click a "Reset Filters" or similar action that clears all filter-related URL parameters and triggers searchCampSessions again with no filters applied (or with default filters derived from getCampFilterOptions).
The header cart summary triggers getCartSummary on page mount (and optionally on specific navigation events). Clicking the cart summary navigates to cart.html.
page_functionality.state_logic: URL parameters are the single source of truth for the camp search state; all filter controls and search queries reflect and update them. On page load: 1) The page reads ageGroupId, month, year, sessionType, maxPrice, startDateFrom, and startDateTo from the URL query string. 2) These values populate an internal filter state object. 3) The page calls getCampFilterOptions. 4) Once options are returned, the filter state is validated against them (e.g., if ageGroupId does not match any age_groups from getCampFilterOptions, the age group filter is cleared). Date logic: If startDateFrom and startDateTo are present, they are passed directly into searchCampSessions. If they are absent and month/year are present, the page derives startDateFrom and startDateTo for that month and passes all of month, year, startDateFrom, and startDateTo into searchCampSessions; the derived start/end dates are not necessarily written back to the URL unless desired, but month/year always remain in the URL as given. If neither month/year nor explicit dates are present, the page calls searchCampSessions with undefined date parameters, letting the backend decide the default range. Filter precedence: Explicit startDateFrom/startDateTo parameters override month/year for the purposes of the search (even if month/year are present); however, month/year remain in the URL and UI until the user changes or clears them. When a filter control changes: the corresponding URL parameter is added or updated; if a filter is cleared (e.g., maxPrice blanked out, ageGroupId set to an unfiltered state), the corresponding URL parameter is removed. After each URL update, searchCampSessions is called with the latest full set of parameters. Browser navigation (back/forward): when the URL changes due to browser history, the page listens for URL change events, re-parses the query string, updates the internal filter state and UI controls, and calls searchCampSessions again to keep the list in sync. No state is persisted via localStorage, sessionStorage, or cookies; the URL and current in-memory state are the only state holders on this page.

components.functionality: Coordinates initial data loading and state setup for the Camps page: parses URL parameters, loads filter options, initializes filter state, and triggers the initial camp session search and cart summary load.
event_handlers: onPageLoad: Parse URL parameters (ageGroupId, month, year, sessionType, maxPrice, startDateFrom, startDateTo) into an internal filter state object.
onPageLoad: Call getCampFilterOptions() and store returned age_groups, months, and session_types in shared state for use by filter components.
onFilterOptionsLoaded: Validate URL-derived ageGroupId and sessionType against the returned options; if invalid, clear those filter values before searching.
onPageLoadAfterFiltersReady: Apply date precedence rules (explicit startDateFrom/startDateTo over month/year, or derive dates from month/year if explicit dates are absent).
onPageLoadAfterStateReady: Call searchCampSessions(ageGroupId, startDateFrom, startDateTo, month, year, sessionType, maxPrice) and store the results for the session list component.
onPageLoad: Call getCartSummary() and store cart and items in shared state for the cart summary component.
onUrlChange: Re-run the URL parsing and state synchronization logic when the browser back/forward navigation changes the query string, then call searchCampSessions again with the updated state.

components.functionality: Provides controls for users to filter camp sessions by age group, month/year, optional custom date range, session type, and maximum price. All interactions update the URL and re-trigger the camp search.
event_handlers: onOptionsBind: Populate age group dropdown from getCampFilterOptions().age_groups and month/year selector from getCampFilterOptions().months; populate session type selector from getCampFilterOptions().session_types.
onInitialBind: Set initial control values (selected age group, month/year, session type, maxPrice, date range) based on the internal filter state derived from URL parameters.
onAgeGroupChange: Update internal filter state.ageGroupId, update URL parameter ageGroupId to the selected value or remove it if cleared, then call searchCampSessions with the new state.

onMonthChange: Update internal filter state.month; if year is already set, optionally derive a date range for that month and update startDateFrom and startDateTo in the internal state (without conflicting with explicit date range if present, according to the precedence rules). Update URL parameter month and re-call searchCampSessions.

onYearChange: Update internal filter state.year; combined with month, optionally derive a date range and update startDateFrom/startDateTo in state; update URL parameter year and re-call searchCampSessions.

onDateRangeChange: When the user sets or changes startDateFrom/startDateTo via date inputs, update those values in the internal state and URL; if explicit date range is set, it takes precedence over month/year for the search. Call searchCampSessions with updated state.
onSessionTypeChange: Update internal filter state.sessionType and URL parameter sessionType; call searchCampSessions.
onMaxPriceChange: Update internal filter state.maxPrice and URL parameter maxPrice (or remove if empty); call searchCampSessions.

onResetFiltersClick: Clear all filter fields in the form, remove all filter-related URL parameters (ageGroupId, month, year, sessionType, maxPrice, startDateFrom, startDateTo), reset internal filter state to an unfiltered condition, and call searchCampSessions with no filter parameters.

components.functionality: Displays a concise summary of all currently active filters as removable items, allowing the user to quickly see and clear individual filters.
event_handlers: onStateChange: Read the current internal filter state and render a list of active filters (for example, selected age group label, chosen month/year, date range, session type, and maximum price). No labels or values are hardcoded; they come from the filter state and from options returned by getCampFilterOptions.
onRemoveAgeGroupFilterClick: Clear ageGroupId from internal state and URL; call searchCampSessions with the updated state.
onRemoveMonthFilterClick: Clear month (and optionally year if no longer meaningful) from internal state and URL; call searchCampSessions.
onRemoveDateRangeFilterClick: Clear startDateFrom and startDateTo from internal state and URL; call searchCampSessions.
onRemoveSessionTypeFilterClick: Clear sessionType from internal state and URL; call searchCampSessions.
onRemoveMaxPriceFilterClick: Clear maxPrice from internal state and URL; call searchCampSessions.

components.functionality: Renders the list of camp session results returned by searchCampSessions, with key details and interactions for each session.
event_handlers: onSearchResultsUpdate: When searchCampSessions returns, receive the array of camp session result objects and render one list item (card or row) per item.

onItemRender: For each item, display camp_name, age_group_label, start_date, end_date, session_type, daily_start_time, daily_end_time, price, and remaining_spots as provided by searchCampSessions. No static text values are hardcoded; only labels and values from the API response are shown.

onSessionCardClick: When a user clicks a session card or its primary action, read camp_id and camp_session_id from that result item and navigate to camp_detail.html?id={camp_id}&sessionId={camp_session_id}.
onSessionCardKeyActivate: Provide keyboard activation (e.g., enter/space) for accessibility that triggers the same navigation as onSessionCardClick.

onNoSpotsLeftCondition: If remaining_spots is zero or below a threshold defined by business rules, the component may adjust the display state for that item (e.g., disabled state) purely on the client side without extra API calls.
onSortChangeOptional: If client-side sorting controls are provided (for example, by start_date or price), reorder the already-retrieved result list in memory without calling additional APIs.

components.functionality: Appears when searchCampSessions returns no camp sessions for the currently applied filters and offers a way to clear filters or widen the search.
event_handlers: onSearchResultsUpdate: Detect when the searchCampSessions result array is empty and show this component instead of the session list.
onResetFiltersClick: Clear all filters in the internal filter state and URL (ageGroupId, month, year, sessionType, maxPrice, startDateFrom, startDateTo) and call searchCampSessions with no filters.

onBroadenFiltersSuggestionClickOptional: Provide optional quick actions like removing only the strictest filters (for instance maxPrice or date range) by clearing their corresponding state and URL parameters and re-calling searchCampSessions.

components.functionality: Displays the current cart status (number of items and total amount) in the header while users browse camps, and lets them navigate to the cart page.
event_handlers: onPageLoad: Call getCartSummary() to fetch the current cart and items.

onCartSummaryLoaded: Derive the displayed item count from the length of the returned items array and the total amount from cart.total_amount (if provided), and render them. No static monetary values are hardcoded.
onRefreshCartSummaryOptional: Provide a way to refresh the cart summary (such as when returning from another page) by re-calling getCartSummary().
onViewCartClick: Navigate to cart.html without additional parameters.

layout.grid_system: Implied 12-column center-aligned grid with a max-width content column for the hero text, over a full-width background image. Navigation items appear distributed horizontally across the top within the same grid.
layout.layout_pattern: The page uses a linear, top-to-bottom flow that mirrors the mockup: a slim, full-width navigation bar at the very top (containing the cart summary on the right), followed immediately by a full-viewport hero (the Camps page-init). Below the hero, the page transitions into a white (or very light) content area where the filter summary sits at the top of the content column, followed by the filter form, and then the camp session list. When there are no results, the empty state occupies the same space as the results list. All primary content is aligned to a centered max-width column on a 12-column grid, with no sidebars.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page uses a linear, top-to-bottom flow that mirrors the mockup: a slim, full-width navigation bar at the very top (containing the cart summary on the right), followed immediately by a full-viewport hero (the Camps page-init). Below the hero, the page transitions into a white (or very light) content area where the filter summary sits at the top of the content column, followed by the filter form, and then the camp session list. When there are no results, the empty state occupies the same space as the results list. All primary content is aligned to a centered max-width column on a 12-column grid, with no sidebars.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The navigation bar occupies a shallow band at the top (roughly 64px height). The hero (page-init) fills nearly 100% of the viewport height on desktop, with a full-bleed tennis image background and a dark overlay. The central text block in the hero sits in the middle 30–40% of the vertical space. Below the hero, the filter summary and filter form together occupy approximately the upper third of the first scrollable section, with the results list (or empty state) consuming the remaining vertical space. Horizontal content is constrained to a central column that spans about 70–80% of the page width (max-width ~1140–1200px), with generous left and right padding to avoid edge-to-edge text.
layout_strategies.content_density: Content follows a low-to-moderate density pattern. The hero is very low density: a label, a headline, and a short description with large typography and ample vertical spacing. The filter summary and filter form introduce more interactive elements but remain airy, with fields spaced using 16–24px vertical gaps and clear groupings. The results list is the densest area, but cards are vertically separated and use internal padding to maintain readability. There are no extraneous decorative elements; visual complexity comes from the images and typography rather than from UI chrome.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The visual flow starts at the hero headline in the center of the screen, then moves up slightly to the small purple label, then down to the supporting copy. From there, the user’s attention can travel up to the navigation (and cart summary) or naturally scroll down. As the user scrolls, the eye is next drawn to the filter summary line (which can use the purple accent), then down into the filter form controls, and finally into the vertically stacked camp session cards. When present, the empty state message is centered and becomes the primary focus in the results area. Throughout, the central column and consistent vertical spacing create a strong, predictable reading path.

layout.overall_description: The Camps page opens with a full-bleed hero dominated by a high-contrast tennis-court photograph, overlaid with a subtle dark gradient. A slim, transparent navigation bar sits across the very top, with the logo on the left, evenly spaced uppercase nav links in the middle, and a compact cart summary on the right. In the center of the hero, a vertical stack of text—small purple label, bold white headline, and a short description—sits over the image, occupying a comfortable column that feels anchored to the middle of the screen. As the user scrolls, the hero image gives way to a clean white content area where a one-line filter summary runs across the top of a centered content column, followed by a neatly arranged filter form. Below, a vertical list of camp session cards stretches down the page, each card breathing in its own band of space, aligned to the same central grid. If no camps match the filters, a simple, centered empty state message replaces the list in that same area. The overall layout feels modern, image-driven, and spacious, with a clear, vertical narrative flow and a single purple accent tying the sections together visually.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The camps-page-init component forms the hero section directly beneath the global navigation bar. It spans the full width of the viewport and extends to fill essentially 100% of the visible height on desktop, minus the height of the nav. A full-bleed tennis-related photograph sits in the background with a dark, slightly diagonal overlay to ensure text readability. In the vertical center of this hero, a single centered content column (approximately 50–60% of the page width, max-width around 720px) contains three stacked text elements: at the top, a small, uppercase purple label (e.g., “CAMPS” or “JUNIOR CAMPS”), centered horizontally; below that, a large, bold white headline that is the brightest, most visually dominant element; and beneath it, a short, one- or two-line description in lighter-weight white or light gray text. Each text element is separated by generous vertical spacing (24–32px between label, headline, and body) to maintain the low-density hero feel. The text is center-aligned within the column to echo the mockup’s strong central axis. Below the description, there may be a subtle scroll affordance (e.g., a faint down arrow or “Scroll to view camps”) aligned to the center, with ample space (32–48px) before the bottom of the hero. The edges of the hero content column maintain at least 24–32px padding from the viewport sides on small screens, ensuring that the text never touches the edges. This component visually anchors the page and sets the tone before users encounter filters or results.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: The hero is static in the document flow, occupying the top of the page content below the nav; it scales responsively in height to maintain a full-viewport presence on larger screens and condenses proportionally on smaller screens while keeping the central text vertically centered.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The camp-filter-summary sits immediately below the hero, at the top of the main content area where the background transitions from the dark hero overlay to a clean white (or very light) surface. It aligns to the same central max-width column as the hero text but now spans nearly the full column width (about 70–80% of the page width, up to ~1140–1200px). Vertically, it forms a slim horizontal band—about 56–72px tall—providing a concise overview of the current filter state. The top of this band starts after a comfortable section margin from the hero (approximately 64px on desktop), reinforcing the editorial, spacious feel. Inside this strip, content is arranged in a single row on desktop: on the left, a short label such as “Showing camps for:” or “Active filters” with a small purple accent (either the label itself or a pill-shaped badge); next to it, inline filter summary chips or text; and on the right, a “Clear all” text link or ghost button. All elements are vertically centered within the band. On smaller screens, this row can wrap into two lines but still stays within the same column and retains generous vertical padding (16–24px). The filter-summary visually separates the exploratory hero from the list area and serves as a subtle, secondary focal point when users start interacting with filters.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: The filter summary scrolls naturally with the page; it is not fixed or sticky. It always appears above the filter form and results, maintaining its function as a contextual banner for the current filter state.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The camp-filter-form is placed directly beneath the camp-filter-summary within the same white content section, aligned to the same central max-width column. A vertical gap of roughly 24–32px separates it from the summary, preserving the airy rhythm. The form itself appears as a clear, structured block occupying the full width of the content column (about 70–80% of the viewport width) but with a subtle distinction from the background—either via a thin border, a very light tinted panel, or a soft shadow—without heavy chrome. Inside, filters such as age group, dates, format, and price are arranged in a responsive grid using the underlying 12-column system. On desktop, they may form two or three columns per row: for example, age and format selects in the first row, dates and price range in the second. Each control has comfortable horizontal spacing (16–24px between fields) and vertical spacing (16–24px between rows), so the form doesn’t feel cramped. Headings or group labels, if present, are set in small uppercase text with the purple accent, echoing the hero’s label styling but scaled down. Buttons like “Apply” or “Reset filters” align to the right edge of the form block on desktop, with enough top margin (16–24px) to separate them from the last row of inputs. On smaller screens, all fields stack vertically within the same column, still maintaining adequate padding (16px minimum) between elements and from the card edges. The overall effect is a clean, easily scannable filter area that feels like a natural continuation of the hero’s modern minimalism.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: The filter form is part of the normal scrollable content. It does not float or stick, but because it is near the top of the page content and visually distinct, it remains easy to return to by a short upward scroll.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The camp-session-list occupies the space directly below the filter form within the same central content column. A vertical gap of about 32–40px separates the top of the first camp card from the bottom of the filter form, signaling a clear transition from control inputs to results. The list spans the full width of the content column (70–80% of the viewport width, max-width ~1140–1200px) and contains vertically stacked camp session cards. Each card stretches nearly the width of the column with consistent horizontal padding (24–32px) inside, and is separated from the next card by 24–32px of vertical space. Cards may have a slightly elevated or bordered appearance to stand out against the white background, but remain visually simple, in keeping with the semi-minimalist DNA. Content within each card is aligned left, following a clear hierarchy: camp title and key info at the top left; secondary details (age range, dates, times) grouped beneath; and critical actions (price, ‘View details’, ‘Enroll’) aligned to the right on desktop or stacked at the bottom on mobile. Typography remains larger and airy enough to scan quickly. As the user scrolls, the list may extend for several screens vertically, but the central alignment and consistent spacing preserve a controlled, editorial feel rather than a cluttered catalog.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: The results list is fully scrollable and grows vertically based on the number of camp sessions. It does not stick or float. When present, it replaces the empty state; only one of them occupies this area at a time.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The camp-session-empty-state is rendered in place of the camp-session-list whenever no results match the chosen filters. It occupies the same central content column beneath the filter form. After the bottom of the filter form, a slightly larger vertical gap (around 48–64px) leads into a centered empty-state block, emphasizing its special status. This block is horizontally constrained to a narrower width than the full column (about 50–60% of the page width, max-width ~600px) and centered, reinforcing focus. Inside, an icon or simple illustration (if used) appears at the top, centered, followed by a concise headline (e.g., “No camps match these filters”) and a short explanatory sentence. The text is center-aligned and spaced with 16–24px vertical gaps. Below the message, a primary action (such as a button to ‘Clear filters’ or ‘View all camps’) appears, optionally using the purple accent to draw attention. Ample white space surrounds this block, with no other list items visible, making it immediately clear that there are no results. Overall, the empty state feels calm and informative, not alarming, and visually matches the modern minimalism of the hero.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: The empty state is conditional and replaces the results list region entirely when shown. It scrolls with the page like any other section and does not overlay or float above other components.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The header-cart-summary is positioned within the global navigation bar that sits at the very top of the page, above the hero. The nav bar spans the full width of the viewport and has a slim vertical height (about 64px). On top of the hero, this bar can be visually light—either transparent or with a very subtle dark gradient—to avoid competing with the hero imagery while ensuring legibility. Using the implied 12-column grid, the logo is aligned to the left edge of the central content area, the nav links are evenly distributed across the center, and the cart summary is anchored to the far right within the same horizontal band. The cart summary itself is compact: typically an icon (e.g., a shopping bag or cart) plus a numeric badge for item count, and optionally a small text label or total. It sits flush right within the nav’s content width, with 16–24px padding from the right edge of the grid column. The vertical alignment is centered within the nav bar, aligning baseline with the nav links. Spacing around the cart is generous enough for a clear click target, matching the ~16–24px horizontal padding that nav items use. On scroll, when the page background turns from hero imagery to white content, the nav bar can gain a solid dark or white background (depending on overall theme) while the cart summary maintains its position and prominence.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: The header (and thus the header-cart-summary) behaves as a sticky element: it remains fixed to the top of the viewport as the user scrolls down the page, ensuring cart visibility at all times. The cart summary does not float independently; it is anchored within the sticky nav bar’s right side.

name: Camp Detail
title: Camp Detail
description: Displays details for a specific tennis camp and allows users to select a camp session and submit an enrollment that is added to the registration cart.

core_features: Load and display camp information (name, description, age range, age group label, location, schedule overview) using getCampDetail(campId).
List all available camp sessions (weeks/options, including full day and half-day AM/PM) for the camp using getCampSessionsForCamp(campId).
Allow the user to select a specific camp session (e.g., a particular week and session_type such as half_day_am) and confirm that it meets age constraints based on the camp’s age_min and age_max.
Show the price and schedule (dates, daily_start_time, daily_end_time) for the currently selected camp session using the CampSession data.
Provide an enrollment form that captures child details (name, age, age group label), parent/guardian contact information, t-shirt size, and medical notes.
Validate that the child’s age fits the camp’s allowed age range before allowing enrollment submission.
Submit an enrollment for the selected camp session via enrollInCamp and add the new CampEnrollment to the cart as a CartItem.
After a successful enrollment, retrieve the updated cart summary with getCartSummary to show current registration items and totals.
Provide navigation to view the full registration summary (cart.html) and a way to navigate back to the camp listing (camps.html) filtered by the age group.
user_workflows:
Workflow 1: View a specific camp and its sessions
1. User arrives on camp_detail.html with URL parameter id=<campId> (and optional sessionId=<campSessionId>).
2. On page load, the page calls getCampDetail(campId) using the id URL parameter.
3. The page also calls getCampSessionsForCamp(campId) to retrieve all sessions (CampSession records) for this camp.
4. The page displays the camp’s name, description, age range (age_min–age_max), age_group_label, location (if provided), and schedule_overview.
5. The sessions list is rendered; the session whose id matches sessionId (if provided and found) is pre-selected. If sessionId is not provided or invalid, the page selects a default session (for example, the first session with remaining_spots undefined or greater than zero).
6. The details for the selected session (dates, daily_start_time, daily_end_time, session_type, price, remaining_spots) are shown in a session detail panel.

Workflow 2: Change camp session option
1. The user views the list of sessions for this camp in a session selector component.
2. The user clicks on a different session row or option (e.g., a different week or half_day_am vs full_day).
3. The page updates the currently selected session in its internal state and may update the URL parameter sessionId to match the newly selected session’s id.
4. The session details panel updates to show dates, time, price, and remaining spots for the newly selected session.
5. The enrollment form is bound to the currently selected session; the UI indicates which session the child will be enrolled in (using session dates and session_type).

Workflow 3: Enroll a child in a selected camp session
1. With a session selected, the user fills out the enrollment form: child_name, child_age, age_group_label (either pre-filled from the camp or selectable if needed), parent_name, parent_email, t_shirt_size, and medical_notes.
2. As the user enters child_age, the page checks that child_age is between camp.age_min and camp.age_max. If not, the form displays a validation error message and disables submission.
3. When the form is valid, the user clicks a 'Enroll' or equivalent submit action.
4. The page calls enrollInCamp with the current campSessionId (selected session’s id) and the form inputs: childName, childAge, ageGroupLabel, parentName, parentEmail, tShirtSize, medicalNotes.
5. On success, enrollInCamp returns an enrollment (CampEnrollment), the corresponding cart_item (CartItem), the updated cart (Cart), and a message. The page displays the message and confirms that the enrollment is in the cart with status in_cart.
6. After a successful enrollment, the page may call getCartSummary() to refresh and display the current cart (Cart) and all items (CartItem[]), including the new camp_enrollment line item.
7. The user can click 'View Registration Summary' to navigate to cart.html, where they can review all registrations.

Workflow 4: Navigate back to camp listings
1. While viewing camp details, the user clicks a 'Back to all camps' interaction.
2. The page uses the Camp data loaded via getCampDetail to determine the appropriate age group parameter to pass to camps.html. For example, it may map the camp’s age_group_label or another available identifier from the camp object to a campAgeGroupId value.
3. The page navigates to camps.html with URL parameter ageGroupId set from this derived campAgeGroupId (e.g., camps.html?ageGroupId=<campAgeGroupId>).

Workflow 5: Confirm age and price constraints for a chosen session
1. When a CampSession is selected, the session details panel shows the session price from the CampSession.price field and the date/time schedule.
2. The page displays the camp’s allowed age range (age_min–age_max) and compares it to the entered child_age.
3. If child_age is outside the allowed range, the page shows a clear error state and prevents the enrollInCamp call.
4. When enrollInCamp returns a cart_item with a price, the page uses that price as the authoritative cart price and displays it in any cart summary section. If the cart_item.price differs from the previously displayed session price, the UI updates to reflect the cart_item.price so that the user sees the final billed amount.
interactions: On page load: Read URL parameters id and optional sessionId. If id is missing or empty, show an error state indicating that a valid campId is required and do not call any interfaces.
Data loading: Trigger getCampDetail(campId) and getCampSessionsForCamp(campId) once the id parameter is available. Handle loading and error states for each call separately.

Session pre-selection: After getCampSessionsForCamp resolves, if sessionId is present in the URL and matches an item in the returned sessions, mark that session as selected. If not, select a default session (e.g., first available with remaining_spots undefined or > 0).

Session selection change: When the user clicks a session row or option, update the selectedSessionId in component state. Optionally, update the URL’s sessionId query parameter to the new CampSession.id using the History API (without reloading the page).

Age validation: On child_age input change, continuously validate that the age value is within [camp.age_min, camp.age_max]. If invalid, display a validation message and disable the enrollment submit button.
Form input: Capture user entries for child_name, age_group_label (if editable), parent_name, parent_email, t_shirt_size (limited to the defined enum options xs, s, m, l, xl, xxl), and medical_notes.

Submit enrollment: On enrollment form submit, if validation passes, call enrollInCamp with the current selected sessionId and the form values. Disable the submit button and show a pending state until the API returns.

Enrollment success handling: On successful enrollInCamp response, show the returned message, update the UI to indicate the child is enrolled (status in_cart), and expose clear actions: 'View Registration Summary' (link to cart.html) and possibly 'Enroll Another Child' (which resets only the form fields while keeping the same selected session). Also trigger a refresh of cart data using getCartSummary.

Enrollment error handling: If enrollInCamp returns an error (e.g., session full, invalid data), display the error message, keep the form data so the user can correct it, and re-enable the submit button.

Cart summary refresh: After a successful enrollment, call getCartSummary() and show the updated number of items, total_amount, and relevant camp enrollment line item details in a mini cart summary panel.

Back to all camps navigation: When the user clicks 'Back to all camps', derive a campAgeGroupId from the currently loaded Camp (for example, based on age_group_label or other available camp property) and navigate to camps.html?ageGroupId=<campAgeGroupId>.

View registration summary navigation: When the user clicks 'View Registration Summary' (from either a success message or a persistent cart summary button), navigate to cart.html (without additional required parameters).
page_functionality.state_logic:
The page’s primary external state comes from URL parameters and the API responses.

URL Parameters:
- id (campId): Required. On initial load, the page reads id from the query string (e.g., camp_detail.html?id=<campId>). If id is missing or invalid, the page shows a 'Camp not found' or equivalent functional error screen and does not call getCampDetail or getCampSessionsForCamp.
- sessionId (campSessionId): Optional. If provided, this value is used after the sessions list is loaded to pre-select a specific CampSession. If the sessions returned by getCampSessionsForCamp do not include this ID, the page logs/handles this gracefully and selects a default session instead.

State derived from APIs:
- camp: Result of getCampDetail(campId). Contains the Camp object and associated detail fields description, age_group_label, schedule_overview. This data is stored in page-level state and used by multiple components (header info, age validation logic, navigation back to camps).
- campSessions: Result of getCampSessionsForCamp(campId). An array of CampSession. Stored in state and used to render the sessions list and determine the currently selected session.
- selectedSessionId: A piece of internal state representing the currently chosen CampSession. Initially set based on sessionId URL parameter (if valid), otherwise a default session. When the user changes the selection, this state is updated. The enrollment form and session detail panel both depend on this state.
- enrollmentResult: After a successful enrollInCamp call, the page stores the returned enrollment (CampEnrollment), cart_item (CartItem), cart (Cart), and message in state. This state is used to show confirmation feedback and to trigger cart summary updates.
- cartSummary: Result of getCartSummary(), typically called after a successful enrollment and optionally on page load. Contains cart (Cart) and items (CartItem[]). Stored in state and bound to the mini cart summary component.

State transitions & URL synchronization:
- When selectedSessionId changes due to user interaction, the page may update the URL’s sessionId parameter using pushState/replaceState so that the current selection is linkable and shareable without reloading the page.
- The age validation logic uses camp.age_min and camp.age_max along with the user-entered child_age. If camp or selectedSessionId changes, the validation is re-run to ensure consistency.
- Navigating away uses URL-based routing only: 'Back to all camps' constructs a URL to camps.html with an ageGroupId parameter derived from the current Camp, and 'View Registration Summary' navigates to cart.html. No localStorage or other persistent client storage is used; all necessary state can be re-derived from URL parameters and API calls on each visit.

components.functionality: Initializes the Camp Detail page by reading URL parameters, loading camp details and sessions, and setting the initial selected session.
event_handlers: onPageLoad: Read id and optional sessionId from the URL. If id is present, call getCampDetail(campId=id) and getCampSessionsForCamp(campId=id).
onGetCampDetailSuccess: Store returned camp, description, age_group_label, schedule_overview in page state and notify dependent components.

onGetCampSessionsForCampSuccess: Store the array of CampSession in state. Resolve initial selectedSessionId: if URL sessionId matches a session.id, use that; otherwise choose a default available session.
onGetCampDetailError: Set an error state indicating that the camp could not be loaded (e.g., invalid campId).
onGetCampSessionsForCampError: Set an error state for session loading while still allowing camp information to display.
onSelectedSessionChange: When a child component requests a change of selectedSessionId, update it in shared state and optionally update the URL query parameter sessionId via the History API.

components.functionality: Displays core camp information such as name, description, age range, age group label, location, and schedule overview.
event_handlers: onCampDataChange: Re-render the summary when the camp data returned by getCampDetail changes.
onMissingCampData: If no camp data is available (due to error from getCampDetail), show an appropriate functional error message.

components.functionality: Lists all CampSession options for the camp and allows the user to select which specific week/session-type to enroll in.
event_handlers: onSessionsLoaded: Render a selectable list of sessions using the CampSession array (including start_date, end_date, session_type, daily_start_time, daily_end_time, price, remaining_spots). Highlight the session whose id equals selectedSessionId.
onSelectSession(sessionId): When the user selects a session, update selectedSessionId in shared state via the camp-detail-init-loader component.
onSelectSessionUpdateURL: Optionally trigger a URL update so that sessionId in the query string reflects the newly selected session.
onSessionsError: If no sessions are loaded or an error occurs, show a message indicating that there are currently no available sessions for this camp.

components.functionality: Shows detailed information about the currently selected CampSession, including exact dates, daily times, price, capacity, and remaining spots, and reinforces age and schedule constraints.
event_handlers: onSelectedSessionChange: When selectedSessionId changes, derive the corresponding CampSession and re-render the panel to show its details.
onCampDataChange: If camp changes, update any contextual display such as allowed age range.
onNoSelectedSession: If selectedSessionId does not map to any CampSession (e.g., after data reload), show a message prompting the user to choose a session from the list.

components.functionality: Captures child and parent information for the selected camp session, validates age constraints, and submits enrollment via enrollInCamp.
data_binding: Camp (from getCampDetail.camp, especially age_min, age_max, age_group_label)
event_handlers: onFormInit: Initialize form fields. Optionally pre-fill age_group_label with camp.age_group_label if appropriate.
onChildAgeChange: Validate that child_age is between camp.age_min and camp.age_max. If invalid, set a validation error state and disable the submit action.
onFieldChange: Update internal form state for child_name, age_group_label, parent_name, parent_email, t_shirt_size (restricted to allowed enum values), and medical_notes.

onSubmit: If all fields are valid and a CampSession is selected, call enrollInCamp with parameters: campSessionId=selectedSessionId, childName, childAge, ageGroupLabel, parentName, parentEmail, tShirtSize, medicalNotes. Show a pending state while waiting for the response.

onEnrollInCampSuccess: Receive enrollment (CampEnrollment), cart_item (CartItem), cart (Cart), and message. Store these in shared state, clear or lock the form as appropriate, and emit an event (onEnrollmentSuccess) to trigger cart summary refresh.
onEnrollInCampError: Display any error returned by enrollInCamp, keep the form data so the user can correct it, and re-enable the submit button.
onEnrollAnotherChild: Optional handler that resets the form fields but leaves the selectedSessionId unchanged so that multiple children can be enrolled in the same session.

components.functionality: Displays success or error messages related to camp enrollment and offers direct navigation to the registration summary.
event_handlers: onEnrollmentSuccess: When notified by the camp-enrollment-form that enrollInCamp has succeeded, display the returned message and a brief summary (e.g., child_name and session dates, if available from enrollment + session state).
onEnrollmentError: Show error states when enrollInCamp fails, including any returned message or validation detail.
onClickViewRegistrationSummary: Navigate to cart.html so the user can see the full registration summary.

components.functionality: Shows a compact view of the current cart after enrollment, including total items and total_amount, using getCartSummary.
event_handlers: onPageLoadOptional: Optionally call getCartSummary() on initial load to show any existing cart content (if desired by overall site behavior).
onEnrollmentSuccess: After a successful enrollInCamp call, trigger getCartSummary() to refresh cart data and then re-render the mini cart.
onGetCartSummarySuccess: Update the displayed number of items and total_amount; highlight any camp_enrollment items if needed.
onGetCartSummaryError: Show a simple error message indicating that the cart summary could not be loaded.
onClickViewRegistrationSummary: Navigate to cart.html when the user wants to see the full cart/registration details.

components.functionality: Provides navigation actions such as going back to the camp listing filtered by age group, and accessing the registration summary.
data_binding: Camp (from getCampDetail.camp, used to derive campAgeGroupId or ageGroupId parameter)
event_handlers: onClickBackToCamps: Derive campAgeGroupId from the current Camp (e.g., from age_group_label or another available identifier) and navigate to camps.html?ageGroupId=<campAgeGroupId>.
onCampDataChange: Recalculate any derived navigation parameters (like campAgeGroupId) if the camp data changes.

layout.grid_system: Implied 12-column center-aligned grid with a max-width content column for the hero text, over a full-width background image. Navigation items appear distributed horizontally across the top within the same grid.
layout.layout_pattern: Maintain the mockup’s linear top‑to‑bottom flow with a strong full‑width hero at the top. At the very top sits the global navigation (outside this spec). Directly under it, a full‑viewport hero shows the specific camp with a tennis background image and dark overlay. In the vertical center of this hero, place the camp-summary as a single, centered text column, with the camp-session-selector immediately beneath it as a compact, primary interaction. At the bottom center of the hero, place the camp-detail-navigation-controls as a row of dots. Below the hero, stack a main content section that uses a centered 12‑column grid: on the left, the session-detail-panel followed by the camp-enrollment-form; on the right, the mini-cart-summary-panel. The enrollment-feedback-banner floats just below the global nav as a full-width, lightweight banner when active, overlaying hero and main content without breaking the underlying layout.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: Maintain the mockup’s linear top‑to‑bottom flow with a strong full‑width hero at the top. At the very top sits the global navigation (outside this spec). Directly under it, a full‑viewport hero shows the specific camp with a tennis background image and dark overlay. In the vertical center of this hero, place the camp-summary as a single, centered text column, with the camp-session-selector immediately beneath it as a compact, primary interaction. At the bottom center of the hero, place the camp-detail-navigation-controls as a row of dots. Below the hero, stack a main content section that uses a centered 12‑column grid: on the left, the session-detail-panel followed by the camp-enrollment-form; on the right, the mini-cart-summary-panel. The enrollment-feedback-banner floats just below the global nav as a full-width, lightweight banner when active, overlaying hero and main content without breaking the underlying layout.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The hero consumes nearly 100% of the initial viewport height, with the central content column (summary + session selector) occupying the middle 30–40% vertically and roughly 60–70% of the page width, centered within a max-width container. Ample top and bottom hero padding (roughly 96px–128px) keeps content floating away from edges. Below the hero, add a clear section break (64–96px) before the main detail/form section. Within the main section, the left column (detail + form) uses about 7–8 of 12 columns (~65–70% width), while the mini-cart-summary-panel uses the remaining 4–5 columns (~30–35% width), maintaining generous 32px+ interior padding in each card and 24–32px vertical spacing between stacked components.
layout_strategies.content_density: Keep density low to medium, echoing the marketing hero’s spacious feel. The hero remains sparse: label, headline, short copy, and a compact session selector, with 24–32px vertical spacing between elements. The lower section is more information-heavy but still airy: group related inputs in the enrollment form into clearly separated blocks with 24–32px between groups and 16–24px between fields. The session-detail-panel emphasizes key facts in a concise list, not walls of text. The mini-cart-summary-panel is a compact but clear overview with 16–24px spacing between line items. Overall, no visual overcrowding; plenty of white (or dark) space around every cluster.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The eye first lands on the large white camp headline in the hero, then moves slightly up to the purple label above it, then down to the short descriptive paragraph. Below that, the eye is naturally drawn to the camp-session-selector as the first major interactive element. From there, the user either interacts with sessions or scrolls down into the main content. Once below the hero, the left column leads: the session-detail-panel is seen before the enrollment form, guiding users from understanding to action. In parallel, the mini-cart-summary-panel on the right acts as a secondary focal point, reinforcing the cart state without overpowering the primary content. The enrollment-feedback-banner, when shown, momentarily becomes the first thing seen just under the nav, and the slider-like camp-detail-navigation-controls at the bottom center of the hero subtly invite lateral exploration without distracting from the vertical journey.

layout.overall_description: The page opens with a full-browser-width hero featuring a high-contrast tennis photograph washed with a dark overlay, giving the typography crisp legibility. Across the very top, the slim global nav sits in a single horizontal band; beneath it, the hero stretches to the bottom of the viewport. In the vertical center of this hero, a single, centered content column presents a small purple label, a bold white camp title, and a concise line or two of supporting text, all left-aligned within that column but visually centered on the page. Directly below this text sits a streamlined session selector, still within the same column, acting as the primary interactive element in the hero. Near the bottom edge of the hero, a row of small dots is anchored to the center, echoing the mockup’s slider pattern. When the user scrolls, the page transitions into a clean, full-width content section on a neutral background. Inside a max-width, center-aligned 12-column grid, the left two-thirds of the page stack the session-detail-panel and the enrollment form as distinct, card-like blocks, each with comfortable padding and clear typographic hierarchy. On the right third, a vertically aligned mini cart summary card remains in view, emphasizing the cart without overshadowing the main information. A subtle, full-width feedback banner appears just under the global nav when needed, temporarily overlaying the top of the hero or content area. The entire layout feels airy, focused, and image-led, with a strong central vertical spine of content and a secondary right-column support, all unified by the monochrome palette and purple accent.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The camp-detail-init-loader covers the entire viewport as a temporary overlay whenever the page is fetching initial data. It sits above the hero and all other content, matching the full width and height of the browser window. The background is a semi-opaque dark layer (slightly purple-tinted to align with the brand accent) that gently mutes any underlying image or content. At the exact center of the viewport, a compact loading indicator (spinner or progress icon) is displayed with a short, single-line status message below it, both horizontally and vertically centered. Generous empty space surrounds the loader content so it feels calm and unobtrusive, echoing the spaciousness of the hero’s central text block.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Fixed-position, full-viewport overlay with a high z-index, blocking interaction with underlying components until loading completes. It scrolls with the viewport content (effectively pinning content in place underneath). When dismissed, it disappears entirely without leaving visual artifacts or reserved space.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The camp-summary-panel is the core of the hero and is positioned in the vertical middle of the full-height background image. It sits in a centered column that spans roughly 6–8 out of 12 grid columns (about 60–70% of the page width) and is horizontally centered within the viewport. Text inside the panel is left-aligned for a confident, editorial feel. At the top of this block, a small uppercase purple label (e.g., “CAMP DETAIL” or the camp category) appears, followed by 24–32px of vertical space. Beneath it, the main camp title is set in large white type that dominates the visual field; allow 24–32px below the headline. Directly under the headline, a concise description or tagline in smaller, softer white text occupies one to three lines, with a comfortable 24px gap before the next component (the camp-session-selector). The entire camp-summary-panel floats away from the top and bottom edges of the hero with generous padding (approx. 96px+ to both), reinforcing the central, spacious hero effect.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the hero section, always centered horizontally and vertically within the hero’s visible area on initial load. On smaller screens, the panel’s width shrinks to maintain side padding (minimum 16–24px from the viewport edges), and the text reflows to preserve legibility while keeping the same vertical ordering.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The camp-session-selector is positioned directly beneath the camp-summary text within the hero, still inside the same centered content column. It spans nearly the full width of that column (around 80–90% of the column width), leaving subtle side padding so it doesn’t visually touch the edges. Vertically, it is separated from the summary description text by roughly 24px to feel connected but distinct. The selector presents available sessions as a horizontal row of pill-like options or small cards, each with generous 16–24px internal padding and at least 16px horizontal spacing between items. The row itself sits comfortably above the hero’s bottom area, not extending below the central text block region. On narrower screens, the session items wrap into multiple rows or convert into a vertical stack while still remaining under the main summary, maintaining a consistent vertical flow from label → title → description → selector.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the hero’s central column. Its height adjusts based on the number of session options, but it never exceeds the hero’s vertical padding; if content grows, it pushes slightly upward while preserving breathing room above and below. On mobile, it naturally reflows, but always remains immediately beneath the camp-summary-panel.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The session-detail-panel is the first major block in the main content section below the hero. It sits within a max-width, center-aligned container that uses the implied 12-column grid. The panel is aligned to the left side of this grid, occupying roughly 7–8 columns (about 65–70% of the container width), leaving the rightmost 4–5 columns open for the mini-cart-summary-panel. There is a clear vertical gap of approximately 64–80px between the bottom edge of the hero and the top edge of this panel, reinforcing the transition from hero to detail. The session-detail-panel itself appears as a card-like block on a solid, slightly lighter background than the hero, with 32px internal padding on all sides. Its heading (e.g., selected session name/dates) appears at the top left, with 16–24px spacing before key session properties (dates, times, level, capacity) arranged in tidy rows or columns. Ample white space around these details keeps the content readable and uncluttered. The bottom of the panel leaves at least 32px of space before the next component (camp-enrollment-form).
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the main content column. It anchors the left side of the two-column layout. As viewport width decreases toward tablet/mobile, it expands to use the full container width and stacks above the camp-enrollment-form and mini-cart-summary-panel in a single column, keeping its role as the first detailed information block.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The camp-enrollment-form is positioned directly below the session-detail-panel, aligned to the same left column and occupying the same 7–8 grid columns (about 65–70% width) within the main content container. A vertical gap of roughly 32px separates it from the bottom of the session-detail-panel, keeping the hierarchy clear but the relationship tight. The form itself appears as another card-like block, matching the detail panel’s visual style: solid background, subtle border or shadow if used, and 32px of inner padding. Form fields are organized into logical groups (e.g., participant info, contact details, preferences), each separated by about 24–32px of vertical space. Within each group, individual inputs are spaced with 16–24px vertically to avoid crowding. The submit/enroll button aligns with the left edge of the input fields and has generous top padding (24–32px) from the last field, making it feel like a clear, final action. There is adequate padding at the bottom of the form (32px+) before the end of the card, preserving the airy feel.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static beneath the session-detail-panel, forming a vertical continuation of the left column. On desktop, it sits horizontally opposite the lower portion of the mini-cart-summary-panel. On smaller screens, it stacks under the session-detail-panel and above the mini-cart-summary-panel, occupying full width, all while maintaining the same vertical spacing system.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The enrollment-feedback-banner appears as a slim, full-width notification bar that runs across the page just below the global navigation bar, spanning the entire viewport width. When visible, it overlays the top portion of the hero or main content without pushing content down, occupying a shallow band of height (approximately 48–64px). The banner’s content is center-aligned within the same max-width layout as the rest of the page, so the message sits in a central column that mirrors the hero’s text column width. The background is a slightly brighter or darker tint of the brand’s purple (or a success/error variant) with high-contrast text, making it immediately noticeable while still feeling consistent with the site’s minimalist look. Vertical padding of 12–16px inside the banner gives the text breathing room without making the bar feel bulky. The message is typically a single line, and if an icon or dismiss control is used, it sits to the left or right with enough spacing to avoid clutter.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Sticky at the very top of the viewport, just below the site’s main navigation, when active. It overlays existing content (hero or main section) rather than pushing it down, maintaining a consistent layout underneath. When dismissed or not needed, it is entirely absent and does not consume any vertical space.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The mini-cart-summary-panel is positioned to the right of the session-detail-panel and camp-enrollment-form within the main content section. It occupies the rightmost 4–5 columns of the 12-column grid (roughly 30–35% of the container width). Its top edge aligns vertically with the top of the session-detail-panel, creating a neat horizontal baseline. The panel is card-like with 24–32px internal padding and a clean, minimal layout: a concise title at the top, followed by line items (current enrollments) stacked vertically with 16–24px spacing. Totals and key calls to action (e.g., “View Cart” or “Checkout”) appear at the bottom with slightly more emphasis. Horizontal spacing between this panel and the left column is generous (at least one grid gap / ~24–32px) to preserve the two-column separation and avoid visual crowding. The overall height is sized to show the most important cart information without overwhelming the page; extra items can scroll inside the card if necessary but the outer layout remains compact.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: On desktop, it behaves as a sticky element within the main content section: as the user scrolls, it stays visible within the right column, pinned with a top offset (e.g., matching the top of the viewport once scrolled into place), but it doesn’t overlap the footer or leave its section. On tablet and mobile, it loses its sticky behavior and stacks below the camp-enrollment-form in a single column layout, expanding to nearly full width while maintaining internal padding and spacing.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The camp-detail-navigation-controls are placed at the bottom center of the hero, mirroring the slider dots pattern from the mockup. They sit inside the hero area, positioned about 24–32px above the bottom edge of the viewport. The controls form a horizontal row of small circular dots, centered relative to the page width, with equal spacing (8–16px) between each dot. The active dot is highlighted with the purple accent color and/or increased size, while inactive dots are subtle, semi-transparent white or gray, preserving the hero’s minimalist look. The controls are small and lightweight, ensuring they don’t compete with the central hero text block but remain discoverable as the eye travels downward. They sit visually apart from the session selector, with enough vertical separation to avoid confusion, functioning as page/slide-level navigation rather than session selection.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the hero section, anchored to the bottom center of the hero background. As the user scrolls past the hero, the controls scroll out of view with the hero content. They do not become sticky and do not reserve space below the hero.

name: Tournaments & Events
title: Tournaments & Events
description: Page for viewing tennis tournaments and events, filtering them by date and status, capturing rating preferences, and navigating to detailed tournament registration while keeping cart access available.

core_features: Call listTournaments with URL-derived parameters (month, year, startDateFrom, startDateTo, status) to display a dynamic list of tournaments.
Allow users to filter tournaments by month/year or by a custom date range, updating URL parameters and reloading the list via listTournaments.
Allow users to filter tournaments by status (for example, upcoming or completed) using the status parameter and listTournaments.
Capture user rating preferences (e.g., ratingMin and ratingMax) on this page and persist them in URL parameters, forwarding them to the tournament_detail.html page for bracket-specific exploration.

Provide a per-tournament interaction (clicking the tournament name or a dedicated control) that navigates to tournament_detail.html with the selected tournament ID and any active rating preference parameters.
Display the current cart summary (item count and total amount) by calling getCartSummary and offer navigation to cart.html.
user_workflows: Browse tournaments by month and status: 1) User opens tournaments.html (optionally via a navigation item). 2) On page load, the page reads URL parameters month, year, startDateFrom, startDateTo, and status. 3) The page calls listTournaments with those parameters. 4) The tournament list displays results. 5) The filter form is pre-filled from the URL parameters so users can see and adjust the current filters.

Switch from month-based to custom date range filtering: 1) User opens the filter form and chooses a specific date range (start and end date). 2) On form submission, the page updates the URL to include startDateFrom and startDateTo and optionally clears month and year. 3) The page re-calls listTournaments using updated parameters. 4) The tournament list refreshes to show only tournaments in the selected date range.

Set rating preference and find suitable tournaments: 1) User enters ratingMin and ratingMax in the rating preference inputs on the filter form. 2) On form submission, the page updates the URL to include ratingMin and ratingMax (in addition to any date/status parameters). 3) The tournament list reloads from listTournaments using the date/status parameters, while the rating preference is retained in the filter UI and stored in the URL. 4) When the user clicks a tournament name or 'View details', navigation goes to tournament_detail.html with id and also includes ratingMin and ratingMax in the URL query so the detail page can use them to highlight suitable events/brackets.

Navigate to a tournament’s detail page: 1) User finds a tournament in the list. 2) User clicks the tournament name or a 'View details' control in that row. 3) The page reads the tournament’s id and any active rating preference parameters from the current URL. 4) Browser navigates to tournament_detail.html?id={tournamentId}&ratingMin={ratingMin}&ratingMax={ratingMax} (rating parameters are included only if present).

Review cart while browsing tournaments: 1) On page load, the page calls getCartSummary and displays the number of items and cart total. 2) User clicks a 'View cart' control in the cart summary widget. 3) Browser navigates to cart.html (no additional parameters required). 4) When the user returns to tournaments.html (via navigation or browser back), getCartSummary is called again to refresh cart information.
interactions: Changing month/year selectors in the filter form updates internal filter state; submitting the form updates URL month and year, removes custom date range parameters if necessary, and triggers a listTournaments call.

Entering or editing custom date range (startDateFrom and startDateTo) and submitting the filter form updates those parameters in the URL, optionally removing month/year, and triggers a listTournaments call.
Selecting a status value (for example, upcoming or completed) adjusts the status parameter in the URL when the filter form is submitted, then triggers a listTournaments call.

Entering ratingMin and ratingMax values in the rating preference fields updates rating-related parameters in the URL on filter form submission; these are preserved across reloads and forwarded to the tournament_detail.html page on navigation.

Clicking on a tournament name calls a navigation handler that constructs tournament_detail.html?id={tournamentId} plus any ratingMin and ratingMax parameters currently in the URL, then performs a client-side redirect.
Clicking a 'View details' button within a tournament list item performs the same navigation as clicking the tournament name, using the selected tournament’s id.

On initial page load and whenever URL parameters change (e.g., after filter submission), a data-loading interaction triggers listTournaments with the current month, year, startDateFrom, startDateTo, and status values.
On initial page load and when the user returns to this page from elsewhere, the cart summary widget interaction calls getCartSummary to refresh the displayed item count and total.
