Clicking a 'View cart' button in the cart summary widget navigates the user to cart.html without modifying the current tournament filter URL parameters (so the browser back button returns to the same filtered view).
page_functionality.state_logic: The page state is fully driven by URL query parameters. On load, the page reads month, year, startDateFrom, startDateTo, and status from the URL. These parameters are passed directly to listTournaments. If both month/year and date range parameters are present, the page applies a deterministic precedence rule (for example, prioritize startDateFrom and startDateTo) and sets or clears parameters when the user changes filters. Rating preferences are managed via additional URL parameters ratingMin and ratingMax that are not passed to listTournaments but are used to pre-fill rating inputs and are forwarded to tournament_detail.html. When the user submits the filter form, the page serializes the selected filters (month, year, startDateFrom, startDateTo, status, ratingMin, ratingMax) into the URL and then re-invokes listTournaments, so the current view can be restored via bookmarking or using the browser back button. No persistent state is stored outside the URL. The cart summary is independent of URL parameters; getCartSummary is called on each page load and after navigation back to this page to ensure current cart data.

components.functionality: Manages filter inputs for tournaments, including month/year selection, custom date range (startDateFrom and startDateTo), status selection, and rating preference fields (ratingMin and ratingMax). On submit, it encodes the chosen filters into URL query parameters and triggers refreshing of the tournament list based on those parameters.

Fetches and displays a list of tournaments using listTournaments. It reads month, year, startDateFrom, startDateTo, and status from the current URL and passes them to listTournaments. The component renders each tournament using tournament-list-item and updates whenever URL parameters change (e.g., after filter submission).

Represents a single tournament row or card in the list, showing its core properties (such as name, dates, location, and status) based on the Tournament data. Provides interactive controls (clickable name and 'View details' button) that navigate to tournament_detail.html?id={tournamentId}, also appending any active ratingMin and ratingMax URL parameters so the detail page can apply the user’s rating preference.

Displays the currently active ratingMin and ratingMax preference (if present in the URL) and indicates that upcoming navigation to tournament details will use these values. This component updates whenever the URL ratingMin or ratingMax parameters change and is used to give context above the tournament list.

Retrieves and displays current cart information by calling getCartSummary. Shows the number of items and total amount from the Cart and CartItem data. Provides a 'View cart' button that navigates the user to cart.html while leaving existing tournament filters in the browser history.

layout.grid_system: Implied 12-column center-aligned grid with a max-width content column for the hero text, over a full-width background image. Navigation items appear distributed horizontally across the top within the same grid.
layout.layout_pattern: Maintain the mockup’s linear, top-to-bottom flow: global navigation at the very top; a full-bleed, darkened hero with the page title and short description centered; then, directly beneath the hero, a centered main content column containing (in order) the tournament-filter-bar at the top, the rating-preference-indicator visually attached to or aligned with the filter bar, and the tournament-list stacked vertically below. The cart-summary-widget remains visually separated and lightly floating in the top-right corner as a persistent secondary element, visible over both hero and content.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: Maintain the mockup’s linear, top-to-bottom flow: global navigation at the very top; a full-bleed, darkened hero with the page title and short description centered; then, directly beneath the hero, a centered main content column containing (in order) the tournament-filter-bar at the top, the rating-preference-indicator visually attached to or aligned with the filter bar, and the tournament-list stacked vertically below. The cart-summary-widget remains visually separated and lightly floating in the top-right corner as a persistent secondary element, visible over both hero and content.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: Use a full-viewport-height hero for continuity with the mockup, with the central hero text occupying the middle 30–40% of that height. Below the hero, the main content column spans roughly 70–80% of the page width (max-width around a 12-column grid) and is centered. The filter bar occupies the full width of this content column as a single band. The tournament list below uses most of the remaining vertical space, with generous 32–48px vertical spacing between the filter section and the list, and 24–32px between list items. The cart summary is compact, occupying a small area (~15–20% width on desktop) and positioned in a non-intrusive corner, mainly consuming attention when needed rather than space.
layout_strategies.content_density: Keep the density low-to-medium, consistent with a marketing-style hero and spacious layout. The hero remains very sparse (title, label, short description). The filter bar is the densest element but still uses whitespace: individual controls are well spaced with 16–24px gaps and clear grouping. The tournament list items are card-like rows, each with enough padding and line height to feel airy rather than tabular. There is no sidebar; all elements are in a single central column to avoid clutter.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The eye starts at the hero’s central page title, then reads the supporting description, mirroring the original mockup’s top-center emphasis. On scroll, the first focal element is the tournament-filter-bar, which acts as the functional gateway to the page. The rating-preference-indicator is visually attached to or aligned with the filter bar on the right side, making it a clear secondary read. From there, the user’s gaze moves downward through the vertically stacked tournament-list items. The cart-summary-widget sits in the upper-right, slightly offset, so it is consistently discoverable without competing with the central column; it becomes a tertiary but persistent anchor point once the user starts adding tournaments.

layout.overall_description: The page opens with the familiar full-width hero: a high-contrast tennis image spanning edge-to-edge, darkened by an overlay so that the central, white page title 'Tournaments & Events' and a short description sit crisply in the middle of the screen. A small purple label above the title echoes the brand accent. As the user scrolls past the hero, the background transitions to a clean, solid surface (dark or light variant consistent with the site) and a single, centered content column appears. At the very top of this column, a wide, streamlined tournament-filter-bar stretches horizontally, with form controls laid out in a calm, even rhythm. At its right edge, a compact rating-preference-indicator makes the current rating filter immediately visible. Below, a vertical stack of tournament-list items fills the page in a scrollable list, each row feeling like a modern, lightly elevated card, separated by comfortable vertical spacing. Throughout, a small cart-summary-widget quietly occupies the top-right corner of the viewport, floating above the content with subtle shadow, always accessible for quick review and checkout without interrupting the linear progression from hero to filters to list.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The tournament-filter-bar sits at the top of the main content area, immediately below the hero section and centered within the page’s 12-column grid. On desktop, it spans roughly 80% of the total page width (max-width around 1040–1140px), aligned to the same central column as the hero text. It appears as a single, horizontal band with a low-profile background (either a slightly darker or slightly lighter panel than the page background) and subtle 4–8px corner radius, visually echoing the minimal chrome of the mockup. The bar’s height is about 72–96px, with 16–24px internal padding on all sides. Filter controls (e.g., date range, status dropdown, search input) are arranged in a left-to-right row with consistent 16–24px gaps, using large, easy-to-tap click targets in line with the mockup’s generous spacing. On larger screens, the controls appear in a single row occupying the left two-thirds of the bar, while the rightmost third is reserved visually for the rating-preference-indicator, keeping it aligned to the same baseline but visually distinct. There is approximately 32–48px of vertical whitespace above the filter bar (to separate it from the hero’s bottom edge) and 32–48px below it before the first tournament-list item, preserving the spacious, editorial feel.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: By default, the filter bar scrolls with the page, but on desktop and tablet it can adopt a sticky behavior: once the user scrolls past the hero, the filter bar pins to the top of the viewport beneath the site navigation, maintaining its full width and central alignment. This keeps filtering controls always accessible while maintaining a clean, single-column appearance.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The tournament-list is positioned directly beneath the tournament-filter-bar, separated by a 32–48px vertical gap. It occupies the same centered content column and width as the filter bar, spanning about 80% of the page width on desktop with a maximum width constrained by the underlying 12-column grid. The list is purely vertical: tournament-list-item components are stacked one after another from top to bottom, with each item taking the full available width of the content column. There is a consistent 24–32px vertical gap between each item, allowing the page to breathe and aligning with the low-density, marketing-inspired aesthetic. The list extends downward, creating a scrollable column; there are no side rails or columns competing for space. Pagination controls or 'Load more' (if present) sit centered beneath the final item, maintaining the linear flow. The background behind the list remains a single, uninterrupted color, ensuring that the full-bleed hero remains the only heavy imagery on the page and keeping the focus on the events themselves.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: The list scrolls normally within the page flow. It flexes vertically with the number of tournaments and horizontally with the viewport, maintaining its centered alignment and max-width constraint as screen sizes change.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Each tournament-list-item appears as a streamlined, full-width row within the tournament-list, visually resembling a modern, minimal card. On desktop, each item spans the full width of the content column (roughly 80% of the page width, aligned center) and has 16–24px internal padding on all sides. The height is flexible, but on average the card occupies about 160–200px of vertical space, depending on content. Inside each item, content follows a left-to-right layout that respects the overall grid: the left two-thirds contain the tournament name in a bold, high-contrast heading, followed by key meta information (dates, location, level) in smaller, lighter text with 8–16px vertical spacing. The right one-third is reserved for primary actions and status: a prominent call-to-action button (e.g., 'View details' or 'Register') aligned to the right edge and vertically centered, plus a subtle status label (e.g., 'Open', 'Full', 'Completed') placed above or beside the button in smaller text or a pill, using the brand purple only when emphasis is required. A subtle 1px divider line or very soft shadow separates each card from the background, but there are no heavy borders, aligning with the mockup’s minimal chrome. On hover (desktop), a slight elevation or brightness shift indicates interactivity without breaking the clean aesthetic.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Tournament list items flow vertically and reflow responsively. On narrower screens, the right-side actions stack beneath the title and details, keeping the same generous padding and vertical rhythm. They do not stick or float; they move with the page content as the user scrolls.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The rating-preference-indicator is a compact, high-clarity state indicator visually attached to the tournament-filter-bar. On desktop, it sits inside the same horizontal band as the filter controls, aligned to the far right, sharing the filter bar’s background but separated from the controls by a small 16–24px gap or a subtle vertical divider line. It appears as a pill or small badge (approximately 120–180px wide and 32–40px tall) that displays the current rating range or preference (e.g., 'Rating: 3.0–4.0') with a concise label and, optionally, a small icon. The text uses the brand’s purple as the accent color, set against a neutral background (or vice versa) to make it quickly scannable without overpowering the more central elements. Vertically, it aligns with the center of the filter bar, preserving a single clean baseline across the band. On narrower screens, the indicator drops below the main filter controls but remains right-aligned within the content column, with 16–24px vertical spacing from the controls, still visually tied to the filtration area and positioned just above the first tournament-list item.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: The rating-preference-indicator moves together with the tournament-filter-bar. If the filter bar becomes sticky at the top of the viewport, the indicator remains in its anchored position on the right side of that bar, ensuring the user’s current rating context is always visible while they scroll through tournaments.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The cart-summary-widget is a compact, persistent element positioned in the top-right corner of the viewport, distinct from but visually harmonious with the rest of the layout. On desktop, it floats above the main content, offset approximately 24–32px from the top navigation (or top safe area) and 24–32px from the right edge of the window. Its width is modest (about 220–280px), and height varies with content but typically remains within 80–140px. The widget appears as a minimal card with a dark or light neutral background, subtle 8px corner radius, and soft shadow so it reads as an overlay, not part of the central column. Inside, a small cart icon, a brief label (e.g., 'Your selections'), and key summary info (e.g., number of tournaments and total cost) are arranged in a simple vertical stack with 8–16px spacing. Primary actions, such as 'View cart' or 'Checkout', are presented as a single, clearly accented button using the brand purple, aligned to the bottom edge of the widget. The widget intentionally does not align with the hero’s center column to avoid competing with the main text; instead, it acts as a subtle, ever-available utility in the periphery. On mobile, it shrinks to a pill-shaped bar pinned to the bottom or top-right, maintaining visibility but preserving screen real estate.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: The cart-summary-widget is sticky relative to the viewport: it remains fixed in the top-right corner as the user scrolls from the hero through filters and the tournament list. It does not obstruct central content; its z-index is high enough to appear above the background sections but low enough that global navigation or modal overlays can still supersede it when necessary. On smaller screens, it may collapse to an icon-plus-badge that expands on tap, maintaining the same sticky behavior.

name: Tournament Detail
title: Tournament Detail
description: Page to view detailed information for a selected tennis tournament, browse its events and rating brackets, and register for a specific tournament event with cart-aware registration.

core_features: Load and display tournament details (name, dates, location, description, schedule overview) based on the URL parameter `id` using `getTournamentDetail`.
List tournament events (including event type and rating bracket information), with optional filtering by event type and rating range, using `getTournamentEventsForTournament`.
Allow the user to select a specific tournament event from the event list for registration.

Provide a tournament registration form that collects player details (name, rating, email), emergency contact information, preferred match time, and t‑shirt size, bound to the selected tournament event.
Submit a registration for the selected tournament event via `registerForTournamentEvent`, then show an inline confirmation and brief registration/cart summary.
Display a cart summary section using `getCartSummary`, updating after successful registration, and provide a navigation action to `cart.html` ('View Registration Summary').
Provide breadcrumb navigation back to the tournaments listing (`tournaments.html`) with appropriate month and year URL parameters derived from the current tournament’s date.
user_workflows:
View tournament details and events:
1. User navigates to `tournament_detail.html?id={tournamentId}` from another page.
2. The page reads the `id` URL parameter and calls `getTournamentDetail` with this value.
3. Tournament information (including schedule overview) is displayed in the header and overview sections.
4. The page reads optional URL parameters `eventType`, `minRating`, and `maxRating` (if present) and calls `getTournamentEventsForTournament` with `tournamentId` and these filter values.
5. The event list displays the matching tournament events, each showing event type, rating bracket, price, and availability.

Filter events by type and rating:
1. User adjusts the event filter controls (event type and/or rating range) in the filter form.
2. On filter change or submission, the page updates the URL query parameters (`eventType`, `minRating`, `maxRating`) without navigating away from the page.
3. The filter change triggers a new call to `getTournamentEventsForTournament` with the updated filter values and the same `tournamentId`.
4. The event list refreshes to show only events matching the selected filters.
5. The currently selected event (if any) is validated against the new list; if it is no longer present, the selection is cleared from the registration form.

Register for a tournament event:
1. From the event list, the user clicks a control (e.g., Select/Enroll) on a specific event.
2. The selected event’s ID is stored in page state (and optionally reflected in URL as `selectedEventId`) and passed into the registration form.
3. The registration form displays the selected event context (e.g., event name and rating bracket) and enables the input fields for player and contact information.
4. The user enters player name, numerical skill rating, email, preferred match time, t‑shirt size, and emergency contact details, then submits the form.
5. On submission, the page calls `registerForTournamentEvent` with the selected `tournamentEventId` and the form field values.
6. If the API returns success, the page displays a confirmation panel using the returned `registration`, `cart_item`, and `message` data and triggers a refresh of the cart summary using `getCartSummary`.
7. If the API returns an error (e.g., validation or capacity issue), an error message is displayed and the form remains editable for correction.

View cart/registration summary from tournament page:
1. On page load, the cart summary component calls `getCartSummary` to show the current cart total and items count (if available).
2. After a successful registration, the cart summary component is notified and calls `getCartSummary` again to reflect the newly added tournament registration.
3. The user clicks the 'View Registration Summary' button in the cart summary component.
4. The page navigates to `cart.html` (no additional parameters required), where the full cart details are shown.

Navigate back to tournaments list with contextual month/year:
1. The breadcrumb component reads the loaded tournament’s `start_date` from the `Tournament` object.
2. It derives the tournament month and year values from `start_date`.
3. When the user clicks 'Back to Tournaments', the page navigates to `tournaments.html?month={tournamentMonth}&year={tournamentYear}` using these derived values, allowing the tournaments listing page to pre-filter to the relevant month.
interactions:
Page load:
- Read URL parameter `id`.
- If `id` is present, call `getTournamentDetail(id)` and, once loaded, trigger initial `getTournamentEventsForTournament` with `tournamentId=id` and any existing filter parameters from the URL (`eventType`, `minRating`, `maxRating`).
- If `id` is missing or if `getTournamentDetail` fails, display an error state and do not attempt to load events.

Event filter interaction:
- onChange (event type select): update local filter state and URL parameter `eventType`, then re-call `getTournamentEventsForTournament` with updated `eventType`.
- onChange (rating range inputs): update local filter state and URL parameters `minRating` and/or `maxRating`, then re-call `getTournamentEventsForTournament`.
- onReset (filter reset action): clear filter-related URL parameters and re-call `getTournamentEventsForTournament` with only `tournamentId`.

Event selection interaction:
- onClick (Select event): set the selected event ID in page state (and optionally set URL parameter `selectedEventId`), populate the registration form with that event’s context, and scroll/focus to the registration form.
- If the selected event has no remaining spots (based on `remaining_spots`), disable or prevent selection and show an explanatory message.

Registration form interaction:
- onChange (form fields): update local form state and clear any prior validation errors for the changed field.
- onSubmit: validate required fields (player name, skill rating, email, selected event ID) on the client, then call `registerForTournamentEvent` with the selected event ID and the form values.
- onSuccess (API response): display the confirmation component with the returned `registration`, `cart_item`, and `message`, reset or partially reset the form as appropriate, and trigger the cart summary component to refresh via `getCartSummary`.
- onError (API response or network): show error messages at the top of the form and keep the entered data so the user can correct and resubmit.

Cart summary interaction:
- onMount: call `getCartSummary` to fetch current `cart` and `items`.
- onRegistrationSuccess event: re-call `getCartSummary` to update totals and item list.
- onClick (View Registration Summary): navigate to `cart.html` using standard link navigation, leveraging the existing cart context managed by the backend.

Breadcrumb interaction:
- onClick (Back to Tournaments): navigate to `tournaments.html` with `month` and `year` URL parameters derived from the current tournament’s `start_date` (e.g., month index and year number), enabling the tournaments listing page to show relevant events.
page_functionality.state_logic:
The primary state driver for this page is the URL parameter `id`, which represents the `tournamentId` to display. On initial load and any time the URL changes:

- If `id` is present:
  - The page calls `getTournamentDetail(id)` and uses the returned `Tournament` object and related fields (`description`, `schedule_overview`) as the source of truth for the tournament header and overview components.
  - Once the tournament is successfully loaded, the page reads optional query parameters:
    - `eventType` (string; maps to `TournamentEvent.event_type`),
    - `minRating` (number; maps to `TournamentEvent.rating_min` filter),
    - `maxRating` (number; maps to `TournamentEvent.rating_max` filter),
    - optionally `selectedEventId` (string; used to preselect an event in the registration form when returning to the page or sharing links).
  - The page then calls `getTournamentEventsForTournament` with:
    - `tournamentId = id`,
    - `eventType` from URL (or undefined if not present),
    - `minRating` from URL (or undefined),
    - `maxRating` from URL (or undefined).
  - The resulting list of `TournamentEvent` objects populates the event list. If `selectedEventId` is present and matches one of the loaded events, that event is marked as selected and bound to the registration form by default.

- If `id` is missing or invalid (e.g., `getTournamentDetail` fails):
  - The page enters an error state, hides event list and registration form components, and prompts the user to navigate back to `tournaments.html`.

- Filter-related state is synchronized with URL parameters (`eventType`, `minRating`, `maxRating`):
  - When the user changes filters via the filter form, the page updates these query parameters using client-side navigation APIs (without a full reload if possible) and re-calls `getTournamentEventsForTournament`.
  - When the page is reloaded with those parameters present, the filter form initializes its controls from the URL values to reflect the current filter state.

- Selected event state is stored in memory and may optionally be mirrored in the URL as `selectedEventId`:
  - Changing the selected event updates this state; the registration form reads from it to know which `tournamentEventId` to use when calling `registerForTournamentEvent`.
  - If the user navigates away and returns with `selectedEventId` in the URL, the page selects that event if it exists in the current event list.

- Cart state is not persisted in localStorage or URL, but is always retrieved via `getCartSummary` when needed. The cart summary component re-fetches the cart after every successful registration, ensuring the UI reflects the latest cart totals without storing cart data on the client beyond the current page session.

components.functionality: Initializes the page by reading the `id` URL parameter, fetching tournament details via `getTournamentDetail`, and orchestrating the initial load of tournament events using `getTournamentEventsForTournament` once the tournament data is available.

Displays core tournament information including name, date range, location, status, and extended description. It also exposes derived values (such as start month and year) for use by the breadcrumb component.
Shows the tournament schedule overview text returned from `getTournamentDetail`, providing users with a concise view of how matches are organized over the tournament dates.

Displays a breadcrumb with a link back to the tournaments listing. Uses the current tournament’s `start_date` to derive `tournamentMonth` and `tournamentYear` for navigating to `tournaments.html?month={tournamentMonth}&year={tournamentYear}` when the user clicks 'Back to Tournaments'.

Provides controls for filtering tournament events by event type and rating range. Synchronizes filter values with URL parameters and triggers refreshed calls to `getTournamentEventsForTournament` whenever filters change.

Displays the list of tournament events returned by `getTournamentEventsForTournament`. Shows key details such as event name, event type, rating bracket labels, price, and remaining spots, and offers a 'Select' action to choose an event for registration.

Collects all necessary player registration data for the selected tournament event, including player name, skill rating, email, preferred match time, t‑shirt size, and emergency contact details. On submit, it calls `registerForTournamentEvent` with the selected `tournamentEventId` and form values, then surfaces success or error states.

Shows an inline confirmation after a successful registration, using the `registration` and `cart_item` returned from `registerForTournamentEvent`. It displays the key details of the newly created registration and a high-level summary of the associated cart line item.

Fetches and displays a concise summary of the current cart using `getCartSummary`, including total amount and number of items. Updates when a new tournament registration is added and provides a 'View Registration Summary' button that navigates to `cart.html`.

Centralizes display of loading indicators, error messages (e.g., when `getTournamentDetail` or `getTournamentEventsForTournament` fails), and validation errors related to tournament loading and registration submission.

layout.grid_system: Implied 12-column center-aligned grid with a max-width content column for the hero text, over a full-width background image. Navigation items appear distributed horizontally across the top within the same grid.
layout.layout_pattern: The page keeps the mockup’s linear, top-to-bottom flow with a strong hero at the top and clearly separated, full-width sections beneath. At the very top (inside the hero region), a subtle breadcrumb sits above the primary tournament hero (tournament-header). Immediately attached to the bottom of the hero is a schedule overview band (tournament-schedule-overview) functioning as a bridge into the detailed content. Below that, a centered status area (page-status-messages) and a horizontal filter bar (event-filter-form) introduce the main interaction zone. The core content area is split into a left column for the event list (tournament-event-list) and a right column for registration/cart interactions (tournament-registration-form + cart-summary-widget). The registration-confirmation-panel occupies the same right column space when active. The tournament-detail-loader, when active, overlays the entire viewport above all components.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page keeps the mockup’s linear, top-to-bottom flow with a strong hero at the top and clearly separated, full-width sections beneath. At the very top (inside the hero region), a subtle breadcrumb sits above the primary tournament hero (tournament-header). Immediately attached to the bottom of the hero is a schedule overview band (tournament-schedule-overview) functioning as a bridge into the detailed content. Below that, a centered status area (page-status-messages) and a horizontal filter bar (event-filter-form) introduce the main interaction zone. The core content area is split into a left column for the event list (tournament-event-list) and a right column for registration/cart interactions (tournament-registration-form + cart-summary-widget). The registration-confirmation-panel occupies the same right column space when active. The tournament-detail-loader, when active, overlays the entire viewport above all components.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The hero (tournament-header) dominates the initial viewport, taking roughly 90–100% of the fold height with a full-bleed background image and a centered text column capped at about 6–7 of 12 grid columns. The schedule overview sits in a medium-height card overlapping or immediately below the hero, spanning roughly 8–10 grid columns. The main content below uses a max-width centered container (about 10–12 grid columns wide) with a two-column split: the event list occupies about 7–8 columns (60–65% width) and the registration/cart column about 4–5 columns (35–40% width). Vertical spacing between major sections (hero → schedule → filters → lists/forms) follows a 64–96px rhythm, with intra-component spacing using 16–32px steps.
layout_strategies.content_density: Content density stays low and airy, mirroring the mockup’s marketing hero feel. Each section contains only what is needed: the hero focuses on the tournament identity; the schedule overview shows key dates succinctly; the filter bar remains compact; event cards are clearly separated; the registration form is short and scannable; the cart summary is compact. Generous padding inside cards (24–32px) and ample section separation (64–96px) ensure that even with multiple functional components, the page feels spacious and uncluttered.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The visual flow is vertical and center-focused. The eye lands on the bright, large tournament headline in the hero, then moves up slightly to the smaller purple label (e.g., ‘TOURNAMENT’), then down to secondary info (dates/location) within the hero. From there, the gaze is drawn down into the schedule overview band, then into the page-status-messages (if present) and the filter bar. As users scan downward, attention flows naturally from the left column of available events to the right column where selections are finalized in the registration form and summarized in the cart. When active, the registration-confirmation-panel and any status messages use accent color and hierarchy to momentarily override this flow, then return users to the main vertical path. The loader, when visible, visually overrides everything with a centered indicator.

layout.overall_description: The Tournament Detail page opens with a full-width, full-viewport hero that echoes the original tennis-themed mockup: a high-contrast, full-bleed tennis image sits behind a dark overlay, with a centered column of bright white tournament text and a small purple label floating in the middle of the screen. A discreet breadcrumb trails along the top-left of this hero area, sitting within the grid but staying visually light so it doesn’t compete with the main headline. At the hero’s lower edge, a wide, elevated schedule overview card either overlaps or hugs the bottom of the image, creating a visual step down into the rest of the page. Below the hero, the layout settles into a max-width centered content column. A slim band for page-level messages appears first, followed by a clean, horizontal filter bar that feels like a control strip for the page. Beneath this, the layout splits into two columns: on the left, vertically stacked event cards with ample whitespace; on the right, a compact but prominent registration panel topped by a cart summary widget. The right column is designed to feel like a steady anchor while users scan and select events on the left. Throughout, the spacing is generous, the typography large and clear, and the interface elements are minimal, leaning on the strong imagery, the monochrome + purple palette, and simple underlines and cards rather than decorative chrome. When loading, a dark, semi-transparent overlay with a centered indicator sits above everything, preserving the clean, modern aesthetic while clearly communicating system status.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The tournament-detail-loader appears as a full-viewport overlay that sits on top of all other content. It stretches from the very top of the browser window to the bottom, spanning 100% of the width. The overlay uses a dark, semi-transparent background (echoing the hero’s dark image overlay) to dim the underlying page but still allow a faint sense of the layout beneath. At the exact center of the viewport, both horizontally and vertically, a simple loading indicator (spinner or minimal icon) and an optional single-line status text are placed, aligned centrally within an invisible square roughly 25–30% of the viewport width. The rest of the overlay is left empty, reinforcing the semi-minimalist, modern feel. On scroll, the overlay remains fixed, always covering the visible area until loading completes.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Fixed-position overlay anchored to the viewport, covering 100% width and height above all other components; disables interaction and scroll while active.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The tournament-header forms the hero section at the very top of the page, directly beneath the global navigation (not described here) and sharing its grid. It spans the full browser width with a full-bleed background image (e.g., tennis court or player) covered by a dark gradient overlay for readability. Vertically, this hero occupies approximately 90–100% of the viewport height on desktop (with a minimum height of around 640px) to mirror the immersive feel of the mockup. Within this hero, a centered content column is placed: it is horizontally centered within the page and constrained to about 6–7 of 12 grid columns (roughly 50–60% of the page width). This column is vertically centered in the hero, leaving roughly 20–30% of the hero’s height as breathing room above and below. Inside the column, elements stack top-to-bottom with generous spacing: a small, uppercase purple label (e.g., ‘TOURNAMENT’ or ‘WELCOME TO’) at the top; directly below, the tournament name in large, high-contrast white text (largest type on the page); then a short description or key details (dates and location) in smaller, softer white text. The text is left-aligned within the column to keep a professional, editorial look, while remaining visually centered in the hero as a whole.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, full-width hero section anchored at the top of the page; content remains centered while the background image scales responsively to cover the viewport.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The tournament-schedule-overview appears immediately below the hero, visually tied to it as a transitional band. On larger screens, it can slightly overlap the bottom of the hero image, sitting like a floating card centered in the viewport and spanning roughly 8–10 of the 12 grid columns (about 70–80% of the page width), with symmetrical left and right margins. Its height is moderate—about 160–240px—allowing it to present the key dates and schedule milestones in an at-a-glance format without feeling dense. The card uses a solid background (light or very dark, depending on overall theme) with clear contrast against the hero. Inside, content is arranged horizontally (e.g., date ranges, check-in times, match start times) with generous internal padding (24–32px on all sides) and at least 24px spacing between logical groups. On smaller screens, the card remains full-width within the content container and stacks its content vertically. There is a comfortable vertical gap (around 64px) between the bottom of this schedule overview and the next section below.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static card-like section that either slightly overlaps the hero’s lower edge or sits directly beneath it; remains centered within the main grid.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The breadcrumb-nav is positioned within the top area of the hero, aligned with the same central grid as the tournament-header but hugging the top edge of the content zone. It is placed just below the global navigation bar (if present), left-aligned within the central content width (around 10–12 grid columns) and occupying only a single line of text. The breadcrumb is subtle: smaller typography, light or muted color, and minimal separators (e.g., ‘›’), ensuring it remains legible over the dark overlay without competing with the main hero headline. Vertically, it sits roughly 24–32px below the bottom of the global nav and 32–48px above the main tournament headline, creating a lightweight navigational cue at the top of the visual hierarchy. It stretches only as wide as its content, leaving ample whitespace around it to maintain the spacious aesthetic.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, inline within the hero’s top region; scrolls away with the hero as the user moves down the page.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The event-filter-form appears below the tournament-schedule-overview and any page-level status messages, acting as a clean, horizontal control bar for narrowing the list of events. It is centered within the main content container, spanning roughly 80% of the page width on desktop (about 10 of 12 grid columns) and aligned with the same central grid used elsewhere. The form presents its primary inputs in a single row where possible—e.g., dropdowns for rating brackets, date ranges, and event types—each occupying a consistent width and separated by 16–24px of horizontal spacing. There is 16–24px of vertical padding inside the filter bar, with labels set in small uppercase type (echoing the hero label style) above or beside the controls. The bar itself sits within a subtle, slightly elevated or outlined container to distinguish it from the background without heavy borders. There is approximately 64px of vertical space between the bottom of the schedule overview and the top of the filter bar, and another 24–32px between the filter bar and the event list/registration columns below.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: On larger screens, the filter bar becomes sticky once scrolled into position, pinning itself to the top of the viewport (just below the global navigation) so users can adjust filters while scrolling the event list; on smaller screens, it may scroll normally to avoid occupying too much vertical space.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The tournament-event-list sits in the main content area directly below the event-filter-form, forming the left column of a two-column layout on desktop. It is aligned with the main grid and occupies approximately 7–8 of the 12 columns (roughly 60–65% of the content width), starting from the left margin of the central container. The top of the list aligns vertically with the top of the registration form in the right column, creating a unified horizontal baseline. Event entries are presented as individual cards or rows with generous vertical spacing: each card spans the width of the column, with 24–32px of internal padding, and at least 16–24px of vertical gap between cards. Key information (event name, draw, rating, date/time, price) is structured for quick scanning, using bold titles and lighter secondary text. The list extends downward as needed, with the right-hand registration/cart column staying visible (due to sticky behavior) while the left column scrolls. On smaller screens, this list becomes full-width and stacks above the registration-form and cart-summary-widget, maintaining comfortable section spacing (32–48px) between them.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the main scrollable area; on desktop, it scrolls beneath a sticky filter bar and beside a sticky registration/cart column, while on mobile it becomes a full-width, single-column list.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The tournament-registration-form is positioned to the right of the tournament-event-list, forming the right column of the main content layout on larger screens. It begins on the same vertical line as the top of the event list, directly beneath the event-filter-form, and occupies about 4–5 of the 12 grid columns (roughly 35–40% of the content width). The form is housed in a card-like container with a solid background, subtle shadow or border, and rounded corners that echo the minimal, modern style. Inside, fields are stacked vertically with 16–24px between them, allowing quick review and completion of required details. A prominent, full-width primary button in the brand’s purple sits at the bottom of the card with at least 24–32px of padding above and below it, making it the focal point of the right column. The card maintains 24–32px padding on all sides internally and is separated from the event list by a 32–48px gutter. On smaller screens, this registration form shifts to a full-width block and stacks below the event list, preserving its overall card proportions and spacing.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: On desktop and larger tablets, the registration form behaves as a sticky panel: once the user scrolls past its initial position, it remains pinned near the top-right of the viewport (below any sticky navigation/filter bars), allowing users to keep registration actions visible while browsing events. On smaller screens, it scrolls normally as a full-width block.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The registration-confirmation-panel occupies the same spatial footprint as the tournament-registration-form when active, ensuring a smooth, predictable transition. On desktop, it appears in the right column in place of the form’s card, aligned with the same 4–5 grid columns and starting at the same vertical baseline. The panel uses a similar card structure but with a more celebratory tone: increased whitespace (32px padding), a larger confirmation icon or checkmark near the top center of the card, a bold confirmation headline, and concise summary text beneath. The primary action area is a small set of follow-up buttons (e.g., ‘View Cart’, ‘Browse More Events’) aligned either full-width or right-aligned at the bottom, with 24–32px spacing between them. The visual density is low, making the success state feel calm and clearly resolved. On mobile, this panel appears full-width, stacked wherever the registration form would normally appear.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Replaces or overlays the registration form within the same right-column area when a registration is successfully submitted. Behaves like the form with respect to stickiness—remaining visible in the viewport as the user scrolls until dismissed or navigated away.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The cart-summary-widget is a compact panel positioned in close proximity to the registration form to emphasize cart-aware registration. On larger screens, it sits directly above the registration form within the same right-hand column, aligned to the same 4–5 grid columns. It occupies a shorter height—roughly 96–160px depending on content—and uses a subtle card treatment with 16–24px padding. Inside, it presents cart essentials (number of selected events, subtotal, and a short link or button to ‘View Cart’) in a simple vertical stack or a two-line layout, with the total amount emphasized using the purple accent color or bold type. A 16–24px vertical gap separates the bottom of the cart summary from the top of the registration form. On smaller screens, this widget becomes a full-width bar or card stacked above the registration form, maintaining its compact height and clear hierarchy.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Moves with the registration form as part of the right-column sticky panel on larger screens, remaining visible as the user scrolls the event list. On smaller screens, it scrolls normally in sequence above the registration form.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The page-status-messages component is placed in the main content column between the schedule overview and the event-filter-form. It spans the same centered content width as the filter form (around 80–100% of the content container, typically 10–12 grid columns) and appears as one or more horizontal alert bands. Each message band is slim but clearly defined, with 12–16px of vertical padding, a subtle background color corresponding to its type (info/success/warning/error), and left-aligned text. Messages sit on a single line when possible, wrapping gracefully on smaller screens. There is approximately 16–24px of vertical space separating the bottom of the schedule overview from the first message, and another 16–24px between the last message and the top of the event-filter-form. When no messages are present, this component collapses entirely, preserving the clean, spacious feel.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the normal document flow; messages push surrounding content down when present and disappear without leaving excess whitespace when not needed.

name: Articles & Resources
title: Articles & Resources
description: Page for searching, filtering, and browsing training and wellness articles, with quick access to reading list and cart summary.

core_features: Load available article categories and popular tags using getArticleFilterOptions and expose them as filter controls.
Display a search input that filters articles by text query using searchArticles with the query URL parameter.
Display filter controls for category and tag that map to the category and tag URL parameters and drive calls to searchArticles.
List articles returned from searchArticles, showing title, summary, category, tags, and published date when available, and link each to article_detail.html?id={article.id}.
Reflect any incoming URL parameters (query, category, tag) in the search and filter controls on initial load and on URL changes.
Provide a persistent link to the Reading List page (reading_list.html) and optionally show a count of saved articles using getReadingList.
Display a compact cart summary (item count / total) using getCartSummary to keep users aware of existing bookings.
Keep the article list, filter controls, and URL parameters in sync whenever the user performs a search or changes filters.
user_workflows:
Workflow: Initial page load without URL parameters
1. User navigates to articles.html from header or footer navigation without any URL parameters.
2. On page load, the page calls getArticleFilterOptions to retrieve available categories and popular tags.
3. In parallel, the page calls searchArticles with empty query, category, and tag values, and a default limit value determined by the implementation.
4. The page also calls getReadingList to retrieve the current reading list and getCartSummary to retrieve the current cart state.
5. The filter controls (search input, category selector, tag selector) are initialized with empty/no-selection values because there are no URL parameters.
6. The articles-results-list component renders the articles returned from searchArticles. If getReadingList returned items, the component can mark which of the listed articles are present in the reading list.
7. The reading list navigation component displays a link to reading_list.html and may show a count of saved articles based on getReadingList.
8. The cart summary component displays the current cart items and total using getCartSummary.

Workflow: Arriving with pre-set filters (from another page)
1. User clicks an "Articles" link on another page that includes URL parameters, for example: articles.html?query=... or articles.html?category=... or articles.html?tag=....
2. On page load, the URL-state-manager component reads the query, category, and tag parameters from the URL.
3. The page calls getArticleFilterOptions.
4. Once URL parameters are read, the page calls searchArticles using:
   - query: value from URL parameter "query" if present, otherwise empty
   - category: value from URL parameter "category" if present, otherwise empty
   - tag: value from URL parameter "tag" if present, otherwise empty
   - limit: implementation-defined default limit
5. The search and filter controls are initialized with the corresponding values so that the visible UI matches the URL parameters (e.g., search input pre-filled, category and tag selectors pre-selected).
6. Articles returned from searchArticles are displayed. Reading list and cart summary are loaded as in the initial load workflow.
7. If the user refreshes the page, the same URL parameters are reapplied, recreating the same filtered view.

Workflow: Searching by text
1. User types text into the search input (query field) in the articles-search-form.
2. On form submit (e.g., pressing Enter or clicking a search button), the articles-search-form prevents default navigation behavior.
3. The URL-state-manager updates the browser URL to set or update the "query" parameter to the current input value and clears category and tag parameters only if the implementation defines that behavior (or leaves them intact if the design supports combining filters).
4. After updating the URL, the page invokes searchArticles with:
   - query: current search input value
   - category and tag: current selections from filter controls (consistent with URL)
   - limit: the configured limit
5. The articles-results-list replaces its list with the new results.
6. If the user later clears the search input and submits again, the URL-state-manager removes the "query" parameter from the URL and triggers a new call to searchArticles with an empty query.

Workflow: Filtering by category and tag
1. User interacts with the category selector in the articles-filter-bar and chooses a category.
2. On change, the articles-filter-bar notifies the URL-state-manager with the new category selection.
3. The URL-state-manager updates the "category" URL parameter to the new selection (or removes it if the user clears the selection). The same pattern applies if the user selects a tag, updating the "tag" URL parameter.
4. After updating the URL, the page calls searchArticles with:
   - query: current search input value (from URL or UI state)
   - category: currently selected category (from URL/UI)
   - tag: currently selected tag (from URL/UI)
   - limit: the configured limit
5. The articles-results-list re-renders with the filtered results.
6. If the user changes both category and tag, the URL-state-manager ensures all three parameters (query, category, tag) are consistent and searchArticles is called with the combined filters.

Workflow: Navigating to an article detail
1. User scans the article list and clicks on an article title or a dedicated "Read More" link inside the articles-results-list.
2. The click handler reads the corresponding article.id from the bound Article data.
3. The browser navigates to article_detail.html with the URL parameter id set to the selected article id: article_detail.html?id={article.id}.
4. Any query, category, or tag parameters are not required on article_detail.html, so they are omitted unless globally needed by the implementation.

Workflow: Accessing the reading list
1. User clicks the "My Reading List" link or button rendered by the articles-reading-list-link component.
2. The click triggers a navigation to reading_list.html without additional parameters, relying on that page to load the reading list using its own logic.
3. Prior to the click, the link may display a badge or count indicating the number of saved articles, computed from getReadingList (number of items in its returned items array).

Workflow: Viewing cart summary while browsing articles
1. On page load, the articles-cart-summary component calls getCartSummary.
2. The component displays summary information, such as the number of items and total amount, derived from the returned Cart and CartItem data.
3. If the user opens this page after having added bookings elsewhere on the site, they can see the up-to-date cart state while browsing articles.
4. If the implementation allows manual refresh of cart state (e.g., via a refresh icon), the component can re-call getCartSummary on user request.
interactions:
Search input interaction
- Component: articles-search-form
- Interaction: User types in the text field and submits the form.
- Effect: URL-state-manager updates the "query" URL parameter, then triggers a call to searchArticles with the new query and current category/tag filters. Results are re-rendered in articles-results-list.

Category filter interaction
- Component: articles-filter-bar
- Interaction: User selects or clears a category.
- Effect: URL-state-manager updates the "category" URL parameter; searchArticles is called with current query and tag, and new category; articles-results-list updates with filtered results.

Tag filter interaction
- Component: articles-filter-bar
- Interaction: User selects or clears a tag, or chooses from a list of popular tags.
- Effect: URL-state-manager updates the "tag" URL parameter; searchArticles is called with current query and category, and new tag; articles-results-list updates accordingly.

Article selection interaction
- Component: articles-results-list
- Interaction: User clicks the article title or "Read More" link.
- Effect: Browser navigates to article_detail.html?id={article.id} using the clicked article's id.

Reading list navigation interaction
- Component: articles-reading-list-link
- Interaction: User clicks the "My Reading List" navigation element.
- Effect: Browser navigates to reading_list.html. Any displayed count is based on data from getReadingList but is not required for navigation.

Cart summary visibility
- Component: articles-cart-summary
- Interaction: Page load (automatic) and optional user-initiated refresh.
- Effect: On load, component calls getCartSummary and displays a summary. On optional refresh interaction, getCartSummary is called again and summary UI is updated.

URL change handling (back/forward navigation)
- Component: url-state-manager
- Interaction: User uses browser back/forward buttons, changing query, category, or tag parameters in the URL.
- Effect: url-state-manager detects URL parameter changes, updates the search input and filter controls, and re-invokes searchArticles with the parameters from the URL so that the list reflects the navigation history.
page_functionality.state_logic:
The page uses URL parameters (query, category, tag) as the single source of truth for the filter state.

On initial load:
- The url-state-manager reads the current URL parameters:
  - query: if present, used as the search text; if absent, treated as empty.
  - category: if present, used as selected category; if absent, treated as no-category filter.
  - tag: if present, used as selected tag; if absent, treated as no-tag filter.
- These values are applied to the search and filter UI controls.
- searchArticles is called with these values and a limit value determined in implementation:
  - searchArticles(query, category, tag, limit).

On user interactions:
- Whenever the user submits the search form, changes the category, or changes the tag, the page updates the corresponding URL parameters via the url-state-manager. The updated URL reflects the current filter state so that the view is shareable and bookmarkable.
- After the URL is updated, the page immediately calls searchArticles with the latest query, category, and tag values derived from URL-state-manager to keep results in sync.

On browser navigation events:
- When the user uses the browser's back or forward navigation, the url-state-manager listens for URL changes (for example, via the popstate event in a typical implementation).
- On detecting a URL parameter change, it updates the internal filter state and the UI controls to match the URL, then re-calls searchArticles with the parameters currently present in the URL.

Other state:
- getArticleFilterOptions is called on page load to populate category and tag choices; its data is not encoded in URL parameters but used to render filter options.
- getReadingList and getCartSummary are called on page load (and optionally on demand) and their results are kept in component-level state without affecting URL parameters.
- No filter, article, reading list, or cart data is hardcoded; all are driven by responses from the provided interfaces.

components.functionality: Centralizes reading and writing of query, category, and tag URL parameters, keeps filter UI and article results in sync with URL, and handles back/forward navigation events.
event_handlers: onPageLoad: Read query, category, and tag parameters from the URL; initialize internal state; trigger searchArticles(query, category, tag, limit).

onFilterStateChange(newQuery, newCategory, newTag): Update the URL parameters to reflect the new values; call searchArticles with updated parameters; notify articles-search-form and articles-filter-bar of the current state.
onURLChange(popStateEvent): Re-read query, category, and tag from the new URL; update articles-search-form and articles-filter-bar; call searchArticles with the parameters from the URL.

components.functionality: Allows the user to enter free-text search terms to filter articles and synchronizes the input value with the query URL parameter.
event_handlers: onInit: Set the search input value based on the current query value supplied by url-state-manager.
onInputChange(value): Update internal search input state; optionally debounce but do not call searchArticles directly.

onSubmit: Prevent default form submission; pass current input value along with current category and tag from articles-filter-bar to url-state-manager.onFilterStateChange so it can update the URL and trigger searchArticles.

components.functionality: Displays category and tag filter controls using options from getArticleFilterOptions and keeps selected values in sync with URL parameters.
event_handlers: onInit: Call getArticleFilterOptions; populate available category and tag options; set the current selections based on the category and tag URL parameters via url-state-manager.

onCategoryChange(selectedCategory): Notify url-state-manager.onFilterStateChange with the selected category, current query from articles-search-form, and current tag selection so URL and searchArticles stay synchronized.
onTagChange(selectedTag): Notify url-state-manager.onFilterStateChange with the selected tag, current query, and current category selection.
onPopularTagClick(tagFromOptions): Treat as a tag selection; call onTagChange(tagFromOptions).

components.functionality: Renders the list of Article items returned from searchArticles and provides navigation to the article detail page.
event_handlers: onResultsUpdate(articlesArray): Render Article entries using the latest array returned from searchArticles.
onReadingListLoaded(readingListItems): Cross-reference article ids with reading list items to determine which articles are currently in the reading list and visually mark them as saved if needed.
onArticleClick(articleId): Navigate the browser to article_detail.html?id={articleId}.
onReadMoreClick(articleId): Same behavior as onArticleClick, navigating to article_detail.html?id={articleId}.

components.functionality: Coordinates initial data fetching for articles, filter options, reading list, and cart summary on page load.
event_handlers: onPageLoad: In parallel, call getArticleFilterOptions, getReadingList, and getCartSummary; pass filter options to articles-filter-bar, reading list data to articles-results-list and articles-reading-list-link, cart data to articles-cart-summary.
onSearchParametersReady(query, category, tag, limit): Call searchArticles(query, category, tag, limit); send the array of Article records to articles-results-list via onResultsUpdate.

components.functionality: Provides navigation to the reading list page and can display the count of saved articles using getReadingList data.
event_handlers: onReadingListLoaded(readingList, items): Compute the number of saved articles from the length of items; update any count badge or indicator in the link.

components.functionality: Displays key cart information (such as item count and total amount) based on getCartSummary, keeping users aware of their existing bookings while browsing articles.
event_handlers: onPageLoad: Call getCartSummary; store returned Cart and CartItem data; render a compact summary (derived values like total items and total amount are computed from the response).
onManualRefresh: Optional handler that, when triggered (e.g., by clicking a refresh control), re-calls getCartSummary and updates the displayed summary.

layout.grid_system: Implied 12-column center-aligned grid with a max-width content column for the hero text, over a full-width background image. Navigation items appear distributed horizontally across the top within the same grid.
layout.layout_pattern: The page follows the mockup’s linear, top‑to‑bottom flow. Above everything (outside these components) sits the global nav. Directly beneath it, a full-width hero-like header band for Articles & Resources uses the same image-driven, dark-overlay treatment, with a centered text column containing the page title and the main search form. Below this hero header, a slim horizontal control row runs across the top of the content area; aligned to the right within this row are the reading list link and cart summary, styled like light navigation items. Beneath that control row, the main content band is split within a 12‑column center-aligned grid: a left vertical filter sidebar and a right article results column, both resting on a calm, neutral background that contrasts with the hero image above.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page follows the mockup’s linear, top‑to‑bottom flow. Above everything (outside these components) sits the global nav. Directly beneath it, a full-width hero-like header band for Articles & Resources uses the same image-driven, dark-overlay treatment, with a centered text column containing the page title and the main search form. Below this hero header, a slim horizontal control row runs across the top of the content area; aligned to the right within this row are the reading list link and cart summary, styled like light navigation items. Beneath that control row, the main content band is split within a 12‑column center-aligned grid: a left vertical filter sidebar and a right article results column, both resting on a calm, neutral background that contrasts with the hero image above.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The hero-like header occupies roughly the top 35–45% of the viewport height on desktop, visually anchored by a full-bleed background image with a dark overlay. The centered content column in this hero (including the Articles & Resources title and search form) uses about 60–70% of the page width with comfortable side padding (max-width around 960–1120px). A vertical gap of about 64–80px separates the hero from the main content band to preserve the spacious, editorial feel. Within the main content band, the filter sidebar uses about 3–4 of 12 grid columns (roughly 25–30% width), with the results list taking the remaining 8–9 columns (70–75%), separated by a 24–32px gutter. Horizontal and vertical padding inside sections and components follows an 8px base scale, with 16–32px padding inside containers and 24–32px spacing between major elements.
layout_strategies.content_density: Content density remains low and calm, echoing the hero’s minimalism. The hero header only exposes the page label, a large headline, and the search form, with generous vertical spacing between them. The control row with reading list and cart is visually light and compact, using uppercase, small typography to echo the main navigation. The filter sidebar groups controls with clear vertical breathing room, avoiding cramped multi-column filters. The results list presents article cards in a single column with noticeable vertical spacing and clear typographic hierarchy, rather than dense, multi-column grids. No decorative chrome is added beyond essentials; white space and the dark overlay hero deliver the modern, semi-minimal corporate sports tone.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The visual flow mirrors the hero pattern: the eye first lands on the bright, central hero headline (page title) and then immediately on the search form directly beneath it. From there it moves slightly upward/back to any purple label (e.g., a small category label above the title) and then down into the body text or helper copy near the search. As users scroll, attention is guided to the slim control row, where the reading list link and cart summary sit aligned to the right, inviting quick access. The gaze then shifts left to the filter sidebar top, following down the stack of filter controls, and then right to the brighter article cards in the results column. Purple accents highlight active filters, primary calls-to-action within results, and subtle indicators in the reading list and cart, keeping the hero headline and search as the most prominent elements while making filters and secondary actions discoverable but not overwhelming.

layout.overall_description: The Articles & Resources page extends the home hero’s visual language into a functional, yet still editorial, browsing experience. At the very top, the familiar global navigation sits in a slim band, with evenly spaced uppercase links and a subtle underline for the current section, floating over a full-bleed tennis photograph that fills the upper portion of the viewport. A dark overlay keeps the image muted so that, in the vertical center of this hero area, a single centered column of text stands out: a small purple label such as “RESOURCES,” a large white page title like “Articles & Training Insights,” and, directly below, the main search form spanning a comfortable width, all stacked with generous vertical spacing. The hero consumes roughly the top third to half of the screen, leaving broad breathing room above and below the central text block. As the user scrolls slightly, the hero gives way to a clean, neutral main content section. At its top, within the same center-aligned grid, a slim horizontal control row runs across the width, visually light and airy. On its right side, the reading list link and cart summary are arranged inline, styled like compact uppercase navigation items with subtle purple accents, echoing the top nav while remaining clearly secondary. Below, the main content band is split into two columns: a left filter sidebar occupying about a quarter of the width and a right article results column taking the rest. Filters stack vertically with clear grouping and ample vertical gaps, while on the right, a single column of article cards stretches down the page, each card using bold titles, short summaries, and a clear call-to-action. A data-loader state gracefully overlays or inhabits the results column with a centered indicator when content is loading, without introducing visual clutter. Throughout, the grid remains centered, the background stays largely monochrome, and purple accents punctuate only key interactive elements, creating a cohesive, modern sports-academy experience that feels both focused and spacious.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The URL state manager has no visual representation on the page; it sits conceptually behind all visible layout regions. It attaches to the page at the document level, listening to changes in the search form, filter sidebar, reading list interactions, and cart summary, and then synchronizing that state with the URL. Because it has no rendered UI, it does not occupy any physical space in the hero, control row, or main content grid, and it does not alter spacing or alignment of other components.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual, zero-size controller bound to the page document; it does not affect layout or scrolling.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The articles search form is embedded in the central hero-like header area, visually replacing the home hero’s body copy with a functional search focus. Horizontally, it is centered within the primary content column and spans about 60% of the viewport width on large screens, capped by a max-width (around 720px) to maintain a compact, editorial feel. Vertically, it sits in the lower half of the hero’s central text stack: first a small purple label, then a large white page headline, followed by the search form directly beneath with approximately 24–32px of vertical space between each element. The field itself is a single, wide input with a prominent presence, using subtle borders and a clear call-to-action button aligned to the right side of the field or immediately adjacent to it. There is ample whitespace above the headline and below the search form (about 64px top and bottom within the hero) so that the form feels anchored but not cramped. On smaller screens, the search form retains its central position and becomes nearly full-width with consistent side padding, preserving the hero’s strong centered axis.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the hero section; it scrolls out of view as the user moves down the page. On very tall viewports, it remains vertically centered within the visible hero area.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The articles filter bar appears as a vertical sidebar in the main content band immediately below the hero header and the slim control row. It is left-aligned within the center-aligned 12-column grid, occupying roughly 3–4 columns (about 25–30% of the page width) with a neutral background that subtly differentiates it from the right-hand results column while still sitting on the same overall section background. The top of the filter bar aligns horizontally with the top of the articles results list, creating a clean, shared baseline. Inside the filter bar, filter groups are stacked vertically with 16–24px of spacing between group headings and 8–16px between individual controls, following the low-density, breathable pattern from the hero. The left edge of the filter bar aligns with the main content container’s left padding, and a clear gutter of about 24–32px separates its right edge from the start of the results column. Overall height extends at least to match the visible portion of the results list, giving the left side a stable vertical block that visually balances the larger content area on the right.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Sticky within the main content area: once the user scrolls past the hero and control row, the filter bar pins to the top of the viewport with a small offset (e.g., just below the global nav) so filters remain visible while the results list scrolls independently to the right.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The articles results list occupies the primary reading column to the right of the filter bar, within the same center-aligned content container. It spans about 8–9 of the 12 grid columns (roughly 70–75% of the width), starting just to the right of a 24–32px gutter from the filter sidebar. The top edge of the results list aligns with the top of the filter bar, sitting directly beneath the slim control row that holds the reading list link and cart summary. Within this results column, articles appear as vertically stacked cards in a single column. Each card extends nearly the full width of the results area with 24–32px of vertical spacing between cards, maintaining the low-density, editorial aesthetic. Cards use strong typographic hierarchy with a bold article title in near-white or light text, a short summary line, and perhaps a muted meta row, all on a light or dark neutral background that contrasts clearly with the page background. A clear call-to-action (e.g., “Read article”) uses the purple accent color, subtle but visible. As users scroll, this column becomes the dominant vertical timeline, with its straight left edge aligned to the grid and its content flowing downward beneath the hero’s boundary.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Scrolls vertically within the main content area while the filter bar remains sticky. On smaller screens, the results list stacks below the filter bar, becoming full-width while preserving vertical spacing between cards.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The articles data loader is visually tied to the results list area and only appears when data is being fetched or updated. It is centered horizontally within the articles results column, using a simple, minimal indicator (such as a spinner or animated dots) that fits the clean, corporate sports aesthetic. When triggered, it either overlays the results list with a semi-transparent dark or light wash and a centered loader or occupies the top portion of the results area with a loader and optional short status text. The overlay version maintains the underlying column alignment: it respects the results list’s left and right boundaries and does not extend into the filter sidebar. Vertical margins above and below the loader adhere to the established spacing scale (around 32–48px), ensuring the loader feels intentionally placed within the content flow rather than an intrusive UI element.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Positioned relative to the articles results list; it appears and disappears dynamically, either as an overlay with fixed positioning within the results column or as an inline block that pushes content down while active.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The articles reading list link is placed in a slim horizontal control row that sits immediately below the hero header and directly above the split layout of filter sidebar and results list. Within this row, the link is aligned to the far right of the center-aligned content container, visually pairing with the cart summary. It is styled like a compact, uppercase navigation item—with letterspacing similar to the main navigation—using either white or light text on a neutral background, accented subtly with purple on hover or when active. There is approximately 16–24px of horizontal padding around its clickable area, consistent with nav click targets. The control row itself is shallow, with about 16–24px of vertical padding, ensuring it feels light and non-intrusive. The reading list link’s right edge sits a small distance (8–16px) from the left edge of the cart summary, so the two form a tight cluster that acts as a secondary navigation group for saved content and purchases.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the control row; it scrolls out of view as the user moves down into the results. On smaller screens, it may stack above or alongside the cart summary but remains right-aligned within the content container.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The articles cart summary sits directly to the right of the reading list link within the same slim control row beneath the hero. It aligns with the right edge of the main content container, forming the terminal point of the row’s visual flow. The cart summary is slightly more visually substantial than the reading list link—often rendered as a compact pill or icon-plus-text block—yet still light enough not to compete with the hero or results list. It might display a small cart icon and a concise text label and/or item count, with the item count or price subtly highlighted in the purple accent color. There is 16–24px of horizontal padding inside the cart summary for comfortable click targets, and it maintains a vertical height similar to or just slightly above the reading list link, so the two feel like a unified control cluster. The group sits above the filter-and-results split, acting as a quick-access summary bar before users dive into browsing.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the control row and right-aligned; it scrolls away with the row as the user moves into the content. On smaller screens, it may stack below the reading list link but retains right alignment and consistent padding.

name: Article Detail
title: Article Detail
description: Displays the full content of a single training or information article, shows whether it is saved to the Reading List, lets the user save or remove it from the Reading List, and provides navigation to related articles and back to the main Articles listing.

core_features: Load and display full article details (title, content, category, tags, media) based on the URL parameter `id` using `getArticleDetail`.
Indicate whether the current article is already saved to the Reading List using the `is_saved` value from `getArticleDetail`.
Allow the user to save the current article to the Reading List via `saveArticleToReadingList` and immediately update the saved status on the page.
Allow the user to remove the current article from the Reading List via `removeArticleFromReadingList` and immediately update the saved status on the page.
Provide a clear control to navigate to the Reading List page (`reading_list.html`) after saving an article.
Provide navigation back to the Articles listing (`articles.html`) while preserving context by passing the current article’s category as a URL parameter.
Display and link to related articles returned by `getArticleDetail`, allowing the user to navigate between article detail pages via URL parameter `id`.
